proposals.title: Local Time Density Theory for Near-Integrated Processes
proposals.motivation: The theory of local time for Brownian motion is foundational for the asymptotic analysis of nonlinear regressions with exact unit roots. However, many economic and financial time series are better modeled as near-integrated (local-to-unity) processes, where the autoregressive root drifts toward unity as the sample size grows. Existing local time limit theory does not directly apply to this empirically relevant case, creating a gap in the toolkit for analyzing nonlinear functionals of such persistent processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local time density process for a near-integrated regressor converges to a well-defined, mixed-Gaussian limit involving the local time of an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process, and that this limit theory can be used to derive the asymptotic distributions of a broad class of nonlinear functionals.
proposals.method: The approach builds on Phillips's recent work on near-integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes and point optimal testing. We will first establish the weak convergence of the partial sum process to a diffusion OU process. Using a rigorous embedding and approximation framework, we will then derive the limit of the sequence of local time measures. The technical core involves adapting and extending the theory of local time density for stochastic integrals and applying it to the rescaled near-integrated process. The final step will apply this result to characterize the limits of sample moments and test statistics in nonlinear regressions with local-to-unity regressors.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first general local time limit theory for near-integrated regressors, closing a significant theoretical gap. It will enable the derivation of asymptotic distributions for nonlinear cointegrating regressions, threshold models, and smooth transition regressions when persistence is high but not exact, directly impacting empirical work in macroeconomics and finance.
proposals.novelty: While local time for exact unit roots is standard, its extension to the local-to-unity case is non-trivial because the limiting process is a diffusion OU process, not a pure Brownian motion, fundamentally altering the properties and scaling of the local time density.

proposals.title: Nonparametric Kernel Regression with Mixed Nonstationary Regressors: Asymptotics for Stochastic and Deterministic Trends
proposals.motivation: Nonparametric regression theory for nonstationary time series has advanced significantly, focusing on integrated or near-integrated stochastic trends. However, many economic and financial variables (e.g., GDP, technology indices) contain both stochastic *and* deterministic trend components. Pre-2026 theory lacks a unified limit theory for kernel regression when regressors are linear combinations of a stochastic local-to-unity process and a polynomial time trend, which is essential for valid inference in applied work.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymptotic distribution of the Nadaraya-Watson kernel estimator in such a mixed-trend setting can be characterized by a mixture of a stochastic integral (driven by the local-to-unity component) and a deterministic integral (driven by the polynomial trend), with convergence rates and limiting distributions differing fundamentally from purely stochastic or purely deterministic cases.
proposals.method: The approach extends Phillips's recent work on near-integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes. We will model the regressor as X_t = δ_t + ξ_t, where δ_t is a deterministic polynomial trend and ξ_t is a local-to-unity process. Using sample-split localizing and functional limit theory, we will derive the asymptotic behavior of the sample moment matrices. The analysis will employ point-optimal testing principles to handle the unidentified localizing parameter and develop a modified kernel function that accounts for the deterministic component's dominance in the limit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified asymptotic theory for nonparametric regression with mixed-trend nonstationarity, delivering new limit distributions and practical guidelines for bandwidth selection and inference. It will directly extend the toolbox for nonstationary econometrics to empirically relevant settings where trends are not purely stochastic.
proposals.novelty: While existing literature treats stochastic and deterministic trends in isolation, the joint asymptotic theory for their linear combination in a nonparametric kernel framework is unexplored. The key innovation is the derivation of a non-standard limit that hybridizes the two classic paradigms, requiring novel functional approximations.

proposals.title: Nonparametric Regression with Discontinuous or Low-Smoothness Functions: Asymptotic Theory for Near-Integrated and Locally Nonstationary Processes
proposals.motivation: Nonparametric regression theory typically relies on strong smoothness assumptions (e.g., Lipschitz continuity, multiple derivatives) to derive convergence rates and limit distributions. However, many economic and financial time series exhibit structural breaks, jumps, or irregular cyclical behavior, implying regression functions with discontinuities or low smoothness. Existing asymptotic results break down under these realistic conditions, especially in the presence of persistent regressors common in Phillips's prior work on near-integrated and local-to-unity processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing local-to-zero asymptotic frameworks and carefully analyzing the behavior of kernel-based estimators at points of discontinuity or low smoothness, it is possible to derive non-standard limit theory for nonparametric regression with persistent data, where the rate of convergence and limiting distribution depend explicitly on the degree of function irregularity and the persistence properties of the regressor.
proposals.method: The approach extends Phillips's work on limit theory for near-integrated processes. We will consider a nonparametric regression model y_t = m(x_t) + u_t, where x_t is a persistent process (e.g., local-to-unity) and m(·) is of unknown, potentially low smoothness. The core technical task is to derive the asymptotic distribution of the Nadaraya-Watson or local polynomial estimator under these joint conditions. The analysis will involve separating the sample into regions defined by the discontinuity points of m(·) and applying functional limit theorems and continuous mapping arguments to the partial sums of kernel-weighted products, akin to methods in Phillips's 'Point optimal testing' and 'tempered linear processes' papers.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first unified asymptotic theory for nonparametric regression with persistent regressors when the regression function lacks standard smoothness. It will deliver new convergence rates and non-Gaussian limit distributions, enabling valid inference in models with structural breaks or sharp transitions. The results will directly extend the toolkit for analyzing nonlinear cointegration and time-varying parameter models with irregular functional forms.
proposals.novelty: While low-smoothness nonparametrics and persistent process asymptotics are mature fields separately, their intersection is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in rigorously combining these two challenging features to produce a limit theory that reflects real-world data irregularities more accurately than existing smooth-function paradigms.

proposals.title: Nonparametric Cointegration in Heterogeneous Panels: A Local Time Limit Theory
proposals.motivation: Empirical panel data often exhibits persistent, nonstationary behavior and nonlinear cointegrating relationships across units, such as in international economic and financial datasets. Pre-2026 asymptotic theory for nonlinear cointegration, including Phillips's work on local time, is largely developed for single time series. A rigorous limit theory for nonparametric panel models with cross-sectional cointegration is absent, limiting reliable inference in applied work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a suitably standardized panel nonparametric estimator of a cointegrating function, under cross-sectional heterogeneity and near-integrated regressors, converges to a mixed Gaussian process where the mixing distribution involves a vector of standardized local times. The associated test statistics will have pivotal limiting distributions.
proposals.method: The approach extends the local time framework to a panel setting with N cross-sectional units and T time periods. We consider a nonparametric model y_{it} = g(x_{it}) + u_{it}, where x_{it} is a near-integrated regressor. The estimator is a kernel-smoothed pooled or mean-group type. The core technical work involves establishing a joint functional limit theorem for the partial sum processes of the regressors and errors across i, and showing the key normalized sample moment converges to an integral with respect to a vector of local times. The analysis will handle heterogeneous localizing coefficients and cross-sectional dependence via a factor structure.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first unified limit theory for nonparametric cointegrating regressions in panels, delivering valid asymptotic confidence intervals and specification tests. It will bridge the gap between high-dimensional panel asymptotics and nonlinear nonstationary time series theory, offering a rigorous foundation for a broad class of empirical applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of panel data asymptotics (N, T → ∞) with the probabilistic analysis of local time for multiple, heterogeneous near-integrated processes, moving beyond the linear panel cointegration literature and the single-equation local time results.

proposals.title: Inverse Identification in Nonstationary Regression: From Local Time Asymptotics to the Regression Function
proposals.motivation: In nonstationary time series regression, the local time of a stochastic process emerges as a crucial component of the asymptotic limit theory, characterizing the time spent by the process near spatial points. While existing work (e.g., Phillips, 2014; Wang & Phillips, 2016) establishes that regression functions are smoothed by local time in the limit, the inverse problem—recovering the unique regression function from this limiting relationship—remains largely unaddressed. This gap is significant for the identification and consistent estimation of nonlinear cointegrating relationships, which are central to empirical economics and finance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under specific regularity and mixing conditions on the regressor process and the error term, the mapping from the regression function to the limiting local-time-smoothed relationship is invertible. This invertibility provides a formal identification condition for the regression function in a general class of nonlinear, nonstationary models.
proposals.method: The approach leverages and extends the functional local time limit theory for near-integrated and tempered linear processes. We will first establish the limiting operator that maps the regression function to the asymptotic distribution of the regression estimator. Using tools from harmonic analysis and the theory of integral equations, we will derive necessary and sufficient conditions for this operator to be injective. The technical core involves analyzing the characteristic function of the local time and employing Fredholm theory to study the uniqueness of solutions. Finite-sample implications will be explored via simulation of the inverse mapping.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous set of identification conditions for nonlinear cointegrating regressions, moving beyond existence results to uniqueness. It will yield a theoretical framework to assess whether a given nonstationary system permits recovery of the structural regression function from reduced-form asymptotics, fundamentally advancing the limit theory of nonlinear econometrics.
proposals.novelty: While the forward limit theory is well-developed, the inverse problem of uniquely identifying the regression function from its local-time-convolved limit is novel and non-trivial. It shifts the focus from establishing asymptotic distributions to establishing foundational identifiability, a prerequisite for consistent estimation that has not been systematically studied in this context.

proposals.title: Inference in Random Field Regression with Nonstationary Error Dynamics: Long Memory and Structural Breaks
proposals.motivation: Random field regression models are powerful for analyzing spatial and spatiotemporal data, but standard asymptotic theory heavily relies on the assumption of a stationary random field error process. This assumption is frequently violated in practice, where errors may exhibit long-range dependence (e.g., in environmental or economic data) or structural breaks due to regime shifts. Pre-2026 literature lacks a unified limit theory for such cases, potentially invalidating inference in applied work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the error process as a random field with either long memory (e.g., fractional integration) or a structural break, we can derive new non-standard limit distributions for regression estimators and test statistics, which are functionals of multivariate Gaussian processes with different covariance kernels.
proposals.method: The approach extends Phillips's recent work on near-integrated and locally functional processes to a spatial/spatiotemporal context. We will model the error field using a linear process framework with tempered or fractionally integrated kernels to capture long memory, and using indicator-based transformations for break points. The core technical task is to establish new functional limit theorems for sample moments, deriving the limiting distributions as normalized sums of nonstationary random fields. Point-optimal and Lagrange Multiplier test statistics will be constructed and their local asymptotic power analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous asymptotic foundation for conducting valid inference in random field regressions when errors are nonstationary. It will deliver new critical values and power envelopes for hypothesis tests, directly applicable to empirical work in climatology, spatial econometrics, and related fields. The results will generalize existing random field limit theory to a much broader class of data-generating processes.
proposals.novelty: While long memory and breaks are studied in time series, their integration into the *random field* regression framework, with attendant limit theory, is novel. The method synthesizes Phillips's expertise in nonstationary time series asymptotics with spatial processes, moving beyond the standard mixing/stationarity assumptions prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Testing the Direction of Nonlinear Cointegrating Relationships with Functionally Local Roots
proposals.motivation: In applied time series econometrics, identifying the correct direction of a cointegrating relationship is crucial for valid inference and policy analysis. While linear cointegration direction tests exist, many economic relationships exhibit nonlinear adjustment. Pre-2026 literature lacks formal procedures to determine whether X drives Y or Y drives X when the long-run equilibrium is nonlinear, leading to potential model misspecification and spurious conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: The causal direction within a nonlinear cointegrating system can be identified by constructing and comparing the asymptotic distributions of point-optimal test statistics derived from reversed regression specifications, where the error correction mechanism is modeled with functionally local-to-unity roots and nonlinear dynamics.
proposals.method: The approach extends Phillips's recent work on point optimal testing with functionally local roots and nonlinear limit theory. We will develop a pair of Lagrange Multiplier (LM) or Likelihood Ratio (LR) tests for the null of no cointegration in the Y-on-X regression versus the X-on-Y regression, where the error correction term follows a nonlinear function (e.g., exponential, logistic). The test statistics will be constructed using a local-to-unity parameterization of the autoregressive root in the equilibrium error, and their limiting distributions will be derived under the alternative of a nonlinear cointegrating relationship. Critical values and power functions will be simulated via Monte Carlo methods.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first formal testing framework for identifying the direction of nonlinear cointegrating relationships, a common problem in macroeconomics and finance. It will extend the toolbox of cointegration analysis beyond linear VECMs and offer applied researchers a rigorous method to avoid causal misidentification in nonlinear systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the concepts of point optimal testing for cointegration, nonlinear error correction, and direction determination into a single inferential procedure. Existing pre-2026 tests for direction are designed for linear systems and do not accommodate the functionally local nonlinear dynamics central to this proposal.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Cointegration Detection via Support Vector Machines and Neural Networks: Theory and Application
proposals.motivation: Traditional cointegration analysis, central to modeling long-run equilibrium relationships in economics and finance, is predominantly linear. However, many theoretical models and empirical phenomena suggest adjustment mechanisms or equilibrium relationships are inherently nonlinear. Pre-2026 methods for nonlinear cointegration are limited, often relying on specific parametric forms or kernel-based tests with known power deficiencies in finite samples. A robust, data-driven detection and estimation framework is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning algorithms, specifically Support Vector Machines (SVMs) with appropriate kernels and feedforward neural networks, can be trained to consistently identify the presence of a nonlinear cointegrating relationship and provide nonparametric estimates of the cointegrating function, outperforming existing parametric and semi-parametric tests in power and flexibility.
proposals.method: The research will develop a two-stage procedure. First, we will frame cointegration detection as a binary classification problem, training an SVM (with radial basis function and polynomial kernels) to distinguish between cointegrated and spurious series based on features derived from their trajectories and estimated error processes. Second, we will employ a neural network (a multi-layer perceptron) to directly estimate the unknown nonlinear cointegrating function f(·) by minimizing a loss function that penalizes non-stationarity of the estimated equilibrium error f(X_t) - Y_t. The asymptotic theory will build on Phillips's work on nonlinear limits and local-to-unity processes to derive the convergence properties of the network's output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, powerful toolkit for detecting and modeling nonlinear long-run equilibria, significantly expanding the applicability of cointegration analysis. It will contribute new asymptotic theory for machine learning estimators in a nonstationary time series context and deliver open-source software implementing the proposed algorithms for empirical researchers.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning is applied to time series forecasting, its systematic integration into the core problem of cointegration detection and equilibrium specification is unexplored. The novelty lies in formally bridging the theory of nonstationary processes and functional estimation with the flexible approximation capabilities of SVMs and neural networks.

proposals.title: Spurious Nonlinear Regression with Nonstationary Data: Limit Theory and Detection
proposals.motivation: The well-known spurious regression phenomenon for linear models with nonstationary, non-cointegrated series (Phillips, 1986) is a fundamental pitfall in time series econometrics. However, pre-2026 literature lacks a comprehensive theoretical characterization of this problem for nonlinear regression models, such as neural networks or flexible nonlinear functions, which are increasingly used for forecasting and causal inference. Understanding when and why nonlinear models produce spurious significance is crucial for reliable empirical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a wide class of nonlinear transformations of nonstationary, non-cointegrated time series will generate spurious regression results, where standard significance tests (e.g., t-tests, F-tests on nonlinear parameters) are misleading, and that the limiting distributions of test statistics depend on the functional form of the nonlinearity and the memory properties of the series.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of limit theory for near-integrated and tempered processes (Phillips, 2024) to nonlinear least squares estimation. The approach involves: (1) formulating a general nonlinear regression model with a smooth parameterized function, (2) deriving the asymptotic behavior of the parameter estimates and associated test statistics under the null of no relationship, using functional limit theorems and local-to-unity asymptotics, and (3) developing point-optimal and locally best invariant tests (extending Phillips, 2024) to detect spurious nonlinear relationships. Monte Carlo simulations will assess finite-sample performance.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first general limit theory for spurious nonlinear regression, characterizing the size distortions of conventional tests. It will yield practical diagnostic tools, such as new specification tests, to guard against false discovery of nonlinear relationships in nonstationary data. The results will directly inform the application of machine learning and flexible nonlinear models in macroeconomics and finance.
proposals.novelty: While spurious linear regression is fully understood, the nonlinear case remains largely unexplored theoretically. The novelty lies in merging the established literature on spurious regression with modern nonlinear modeling and recent advances in the limit theory for processes with local-to-unity and tempered dynamics.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Theory for the Trend IV Estimator with Non-Spherical Disturbances
proposals.motivation: The Trend IV estimator, developed for cointegrated systems with irrelevant instruments, provides a powerful tool for handling persistent regressors. However, its established asymptotic theory crucially relies on the assumption of spherical errors (i.i.d. or homoskedastic). This is a severe limitation for empirical applications in economics and finance, where heteroskedasticity and serial correlation are the rule, not the exception. Extending the theory to accommodate such non-spherical disturbances is essential for valid inference and the practical utility of the estimator.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Trend IV estimator, under suitable regularity conditions, remains consistent and converges to a mixed Gaussian limit even in the presence of conditionally heteroskedastic and/or serially correlated errors, enabling the use of HAC-type robust standard errors for hypothesis testing.
proposals.method: We will employ a functional limit theory approach, modeling the disturbances as a martingale difference sequence or a near-epoch dependent process to allow for general forms of heterogeneity and dependence. The analysis will involve establishing a new weak convergence result for the sample moment conditions, leveraging the local-to-unity framework for the instrument's generating process. The key technical steps will involve proving a martingale central limit theorem for the score vector and applying a Beveridge-Nelson decomposition to handle the serial correlation, leading to a mixed normal asymptotic distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete asymptotic distribution theory for the Trend IV estimator under empirically relevant error structures. It will deliver a practical, robust covariance matrix estimator, enabling reliable t-tests and Wald tests in cointegrated systems where instruments are weak or irrelevant. This bridges a critical gap between the estimator's theoretical elegance and its applied econometric implementation.
proposals.novelty: While HAC robust inference is standard for many estimators, deriving it for the Trend IV estimator is non-trivial due to the nonlinear, local-to-unity nature of the instrument and its interaction with the non-spherical error process, requiring novel limit arguments beyond standard GMM treatments.

proposals.title: Finite-Sample Refinements for the Trend Instrumental Variable Estimator in Cointegrated Systems
proposals.motivation: The Trend IV estimator, developed by Phillips (2014, 2015), provides optimal estimation in cointegrated systems with irrelevant or weak instruments, but its asymptotic theory relies on large-sample approximations. In applied macroeconomics and finance, researchers often work with very short time series (e.g., post-war quarterly data, T<100), where asymptotic distributions can provide poor approximations, leading to biased inference and unreliable confidence intervals.
proposals.hypothesis: A carefully designed bootstrap procedure, or a direct correction based on Edgeworth expansion, can significantly reduce the finite-sample bias and size distortion of the Trend IV estimator, providing more reliable inference in empirically relevant sample sizes.
proposals.method: We will derive an explicit second-order bias expansion for the Trend IV estimator using local-to-unity and functional limit theory, analogous to Phillips' (2012) work on near-integrated processes. This analytic correction will be our benchmark. The primary methodological innovation will be the design of a recursive-design wild bootstrap, where the bootstrap DGP replicates the local-to-unity persistence and cointegration structure of the original system. The bootstrap will be implemented by resampling from the estimated system of equations, preserving the functional local behavior of the stochastic trends, and its validity will be established using the sample path properties of the limiting Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first dedicated finite-sample correction for the Trend IV class of estimators, making them practically usable for short time series common in applied work. It will extend the optimal estimation framework for cointegrated systems into the small-sample domain, bridging a gap between asymptotic optimality and empirical reliability.
proposals.novelty: While bootstraps exist for standard cointegration estimators, none are tailored to the Trend IV estimator's specific asymptotic framework involving functionally local-to-unity roots and irrelevant instruments. The proposed method directly leverages Phillips' recent theoretical work on point-optimal testing and local processes to control the bootstrap's higher-order properties.

proposals.title: A Residual-Based Test for Cointegration Using Trend Projections
proposals.motivation: Standard cointegration tests (e.g., Engle-Granger, Johansen) often suffer from low power in finite samples and sensitivity to nuisance parameters under the null of no cointegration. Pre-2026 research on 'near integrated' and 'functionally local to unity' processes highlights the need for tests with better local asymptotic properties and robustness. A test constructed from trend projection residuals offers a novel avenue to address these power and size issues by exploiting the distinct stochastic properties of cointegrated versus spurious regression residuals.
proposals.hypothesis: The residuals obtained from projecting a vector time series onto a deterministic trend (or a set of trending functions) will, under the null of no cointegration, possess a specific stochastic structure (e.g., a unit root or near-integrated process) that can be distinguished from the stationary structure present under the alternative of cointegration.
proposals.method: The approach involves projecting the multivariate time series onto a flexible trend basis (e.g., polynomials, splines) to obtain projection residuals. We will derive the asymptotic distribution of a unit root or stationarity test statistic (e.g., a modified Dickey-Fuller or KPSS-type test) applied to these residuals under the null of no cointegration, allowing for general linear processes and potentially 'functionally local' deviations from unity. Point-optimal testing principles, as in Phillips's recent work, will be employed to maximize power against relevant alternatives. The limit theory will leverage results on near-integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a new, computationally simple test for cointegration with a well-defined asymptotic null distribution, potentially offering improved power in finite samples and robustness to certain deterministic trend misspecifications. It will extend the toolkit for cointegration analysis by formally linking the literature on trend projection, residual-based testing, and the local-to-unity asymptotic theory developed by Phillips and others.
proposals.novelty: While residual-based cointegration tests are standard, using residuals from a preliminary *trend projection* (rather than a regression on other stochastic variables) as the basis for the test is a novel inversion of the typical procedure. This approach directly targets the stochastic trend component, aligning with recent advances in modeling functionally local deviations from unity.

proposals.title: Optimal Detrending with Irrelevant Nonstationary Instruments: Theory and Inference for Trend-Stationary Models
proposals.motivation: In time series econometrics, distinguishing trend-stationary (I(0) with trend) from difference-stationary (I(1)) processes is fundamental. Standard detrending (OLS) is inefficient under serial correlation, and unit root tests have low power against persistent alternatives. The concept of 'irrelevant instruments' has been successfully applied in cointegrated (I(1)) systems to improve estimation efficiency, but its potential for the dual problem—using nonstationary instruments to aid in the efficient estimation and testing of trend-stationary models—remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Using deliberately irrelevant instruments, such as I(1) or mildly explosive processes, in an instrumental variable (IV) framework for detrending can yield estimators with superior asymptotic properties (e.g., smaller asymptotic variance) compared to OLS, and can form the basis of more powerful tests for deterministic trends against stochastic trend alternatives.
proposals.method: The approach builds on Phillips's recent work on irrelevant instruments in cointegrated systems. We will develop the asymptotic theory for IV estimators of deterministic trends where the instruments are generated by near-integrated or explosive processes, functionally local to unity. The method involves: 1) Specifying the instrument process with a localizing parameter, 2) Deriving the limiting distributions of the IV trend estimator and associated t-statistics using functional central limit theory and local-to-unity asymptotics, 3) Characterizing the optimal choice of the instrument's persistence to minimize the asymptotic variance, and 4) Designing point-optimal tests for a linear trend based on this framework, comparing their local asymptotic power to Dickey-Fuller-type and OLS-based tests.
proposals.contribution: This research will extend the irrelevant instruments literature to a new, dual domain, providing a novel toolkit for efficient trend estimation and powerful trend-stationarity testing. It will offer a formal theoretical bridge between the literature on optimal instrumental variables and the problem of deterministic trend inference, potentially leading to new diagnostic procedures for persistent economic time series.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the intentional use of nonstationary (I(1) or explosive) processes as instruments for estimating a stationary component, which is the conceptual inverse of the established cointegration application. This inverts the standard paradigm where instruments are required to be exogenous and relevant, exploring efficiency gains from controlled irrelevance and nonstationarity.

proposals.title: Point Optimal and Local-to-Unity Testing Under Long-Range Dependent Errors
proposals.motivation: Much of the asymptotic theory for unit root and cointegration tests, including Phillips's work on near-integrated processes and point optimal tests, relies on weak dependence assumptions for the error process. This is a significant limitation, as many economic and financial time series exhibit long memory or strong dependence. Existing tests can suffer from severe size distortions and power loss when applied to such data, leading to spurious inference about stochastic trends and cointegrating relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the error process as a fractionally integrated (I(d)) or tempered linear process with a fractional parameter, the limiting distributions of point optimal and locally optimal tests for a root near unity can be derived, leading to tests that are robust to a spectrum of dependence from weak to strong.
proposals.method: The approach will extend the functional local-to-unity framework to innovations following a fractional ARIMA or a tempered linear process with long memory. We will derive the limiting distributions of test statistics using continuous mapping theorems applied to fractional Brownian motion, building on Jeganathan's (2008) and Phillips's (2007) work on linear processes. The core technical task is to establish the weak convergence of sample moments and the test statistics under this generalized dependence structure. The estimation of the fractional integration parameter 'd' will be integrated into a feasible point optimal testing procedure, potentially using a two-step or joint estimation approach.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified asymptotic theory for unit root and cointegration testing under general dependence, subsuming both the standard weak dependence and long memory cases. It will deliver new, robust testing procedures with correct size and improved power for a broader class of empirically relevant time series. This directly extends the applicability of Phillips's optimal testing paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While long memory in errors has been studied, integrating it into the *point optimal* and *functional local-to-unity* testing frameworks—which are designed for high power against specific alternatives—is novel. The analogy to seismological models inspires a parameterization that treats dependence strength as a continuous, estimable feature of the error process, rather than a nuisance to be differenced away.

proposals.title: Testing for Structural Breaks in Cointegration Rank: A Robust Iterative Procedure with Narrative Validation
proposals.motivation: Macroeconomic cointegrating relationships, such as money demand or fiscal policy rules, are theoretically central but empirically unstable due to policy regime shifts. Pre-2026 literature on structural breaks in cointegration vectors is extensive, but methods for detecting breaks in the cointegration *space* itself—the number of stable long-run relationships—are less developed and often rely on restrictive assumptions. This gap limits the ability of empirical researchers to reliably date fundamental changes in macroeconomic policy regimes from data.
proposals.hypothesis: The cointegration rank within key macroeconomic systems is subject to discrete structural breaks that can be consistently identified through an iterative testing procedure, and the timing of these breaks will align with documented major institutional and policy changes, providing a statistically disciplined framework for narrative economic history.
proposals.method: The methodology builds on Phillips's work on near-integrated processes and optimal testing. We propose a sequential algorithm that iteratively applies a robust, point-optimal rank test (extending Phillips's work on functionally local roots) to subsamples of the data to identify potential breakpoints in the cointegration rank. For each candidate break, a sup-type test statistic is computed. Upon identifying a break date, the sample is split, and the procedure repeats within each new segment. The final set of breakpoints will then be used to periodize the data for a narrative analysis, correlating them with historical policy records.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified, operational toolkit for detecting regime shifts in long-run economic equilibria, directly applicable to policy analysis. It will contribute to econometric theory by developing the limiting distribution of the iterative rank-break test statistics under local alternatives. The synthesis of rigorous break detection with narrative history offers a novel paradigm for empirical macroeconomics that grounds historical interpretation in formal statistical evidence.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of an iterative, robust rank-break detection algorithm with a structured narrative validation approach is novel. While break tests for coefficients exist, a procedure focused specifically on the cointegration *space* and designed to guide historical economic analysis represents a significant departure from purely statistical or purely narrative traditions.

proposals.title: Robust Inference for Stochastic Trends in the Presence of Uncertain Deterministic Components: A Union-of-Models Approach
proposals.motivation: Empirical time series analysis often involves disentangling stochastic trends from deterministic components like broken trends or smooth transitions. Pre-testing for these deterministic features, however, induces pre-test bias and invalidates standard inference on the stochastic parameters, such as the autoregressive root or cointegrating vector. Existing methods typically condition on a single, pre-selected deterministic model, which is fragile to misspecification.
proposals.hypothesis: By conducting inference over a union of plausible deterministic specifications—such as models with breaks at unknown dates or of unknown functional form—and employing robust procedures like weighted averaging or intersection-union tests, we can obtain valid confidence sets for stochastic parameters that are robust to deterministic model uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will develop a semiparametric framework where the deterministic component is modeled as belonging to a finite-dimensional, data-driven set of candidate functions (e.g., polynomials, broken trends, Fourier terms). For inference on the local-to-unity parameter or cointegration vector, we will construct a test statistic that is the supremum (or a carefully weighted average) of statistics computed across all candidate deterministic models. The asymptotic distribution will be derived using limit theory for near-integrated processes under local alternatives, extending Phillips's work on point-optimal testing with functionally local roots. Critical values will be obtained via simulation or bootstrap methods robust to the presence of nuisance parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical methodology for robust model selection and inference in nonstationary time series, directly addressing the problem of pre-test bias. It will extend the theoretical toolbox for cointegrated and near-integrated systems by delivering valid confidence intervals that account for uncertainty in both the stochastic and deterministic components.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats deterministic components as either known or estimated with certainty, this approach explicitly formalizes deterministic model uncertainty as an integral part of the inference problem, using a union-of-models construction to ensure robustness.

proposals.title: Semiparametric Model Selection and Inference with Deterministic Trends and Breaks
proposals.motivation: Semiparametric frameworks for cointegration and near-integrated processes, such as those developed by Phillips and others, often assume a known deterministic component or rely on preliminary detrending. In practice, economic time series exhibit unknown deterministic trends, structural breaks, or polynomial components. Ignoring these features can lead to severe size distortions in unit root tests and biased estimates in cointegrated systems, undermining empirical conclusions drawn from the asymptotic theory.
proposals.hypothesis: A semiparametric model selection procedure that jointly accounts for stochastic persistence (local-to-unity roots) and an unknown deterministic component (e.g., broken trends) can be developed, yielding point-optimal tests and estimators with correct size and improved power in finite samples.
proposals.method: The approach extends the 'point optimal testing with roots that are functionally local to unity' framework. We will incorporate a parametric family for the deterministic component (e.g., broken polynomials) into the likelihood. A two-step semiparametric procedure will be designed: first, a generalized least squares detrending step conditional on a localizing parameter for the autoregressive root; second, a model selection criterion (like an information criterion or a weighted average risk criterion) will choose among competing deterministic specifications and persistence parameters. The limit theory will be derived using functional central limit theorems for the resulting detrended sample moments, accounting for the interaction between trend estimation and the local-to-unity asymptotics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified asymptotic theory for inference in cointegrated and near-integrated systems with uncertain deterministic trends. It will deliver practical, point-optimal testing procedures that are robust to trend misspecification and breaks. The work will bridge the gap between parametric trend-break literature and semiparametric persistence modeling.
proposals.novelty: While deterministic components have been studied in parametric unit root models, their formal integration into the semiparametric 'functionally local-to-unity' framework for joint model selection and inference is novel. The key innovation is the development of a selection rule that operates on the composite hypothesis of both the stochastic and deterministic components without prior knowledge of the break date or trend form.

proposals.title: Limit Theory for Semiparametric Reduced Rank Regression with Multiple Lags
proposals.motivation: Semiparametric reduced rank regression (RRR) is a cornerstone for modeling cointegrated systems with persistent, nonstationary errors, as developed by Phillips (2014, *Econometric Theory*). However, its established asymptotic theory is largely confined to single-lag specifications, which limits its application to richer dynamic structures common in empirical macroeconomics and finance. Extending this framework to multiple lags is essential for aligning theoretical tools with practical model-building needs where dynamic adjustments and lagged effects are crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymptotic distributions of the semiparametric RRR estimators and associated rank tests, when derived under a multiple-lag specification, will be functionals of multivariate Ornstein-Uhlenbeck and standard Brownian motion processes, but with a more complex dependence structure on the lag polynomial and the long-run variance matrix. The limiting theory will remain pivotal but require a novel characterization of the stochastic integrals involved.
proposals.method: The approach will extend the local-to-unity and functional limit theory framework used in Phillips's recent work on near-integrated processes. We will specify a vector error correction model (VECM) with multiple lags in the cointegrating relations and a semiparametric treatment of the error process. The core technical steps involve: (1) deriving a new functional representation of the sample moment matrices as partial sums of the lagged regressors, (2) applying a multivariate invariance principle to establish weak convergence to stochastic integrals involving a vector Brownian motion and its lagged transformations, and (3) employing eigenvalue perturbation theory to characterize the limiting distributions of the eigenvalues used for rank testing and the estimated cointegration space.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first complete asymptotic theory for semiparametric RRR with multiple lags, enabling valid inference in a much broader class of dynamic cointegrated systems. It will yield new, practically applicable limiting distributions for cointegration rank tests and estimators, directly impacting empirical work in macroeconometrics and financial econometrics that relies on lag-augmented VECMs.
proposals.novelty: While single-lag RRR theory is well-established, the extension to multiple lags is non-trivial because the lag structure introduces a complex, self-referential dependence in the limiting stochastic integrals that cannot be handled by existing proofs. The novelty lies in rigorously characterizing this dependence and its impact on the asymptotic eigenstructure, a problem not addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Regime Validation Protocol for Cointegration Analysis: An Analogy from Species Distribution Modeling
proposals.motivation: Empirical applications of cointegration methods often rely on single-sample case studies, which provide limited evidence of a method's robustness and practical utility across different data-generating processes. This is analogous to the problem in ecology where a Species Distribution Model (SDM) validated on one spatial scale may fail to transfer to another. In macroeconomics, where data are nonstationary and subject to structural breaks, a more rigorous, generalizable validation framework is needed to assess the reliability of cointegration and near-integration inference in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: That cointegration and near-integration testing and estimation procedures, particularly those involving local-to-unity asymptotics and functional coefficients, will demonstrate significantly more robust and transferable performance when validated across a structured battery of empirical regimes—mirroring the multi-scale validation of SDMs—compared to standard single-sample evaluations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the spatial block k-fold validation protocol from SDM ecology to a temporal/regime-based context. Specifically, we will apply Phillips's recent methods (e.g., point optimal testing with functionally local roots) to a curated macro panel. The validation battery will: (1) partition data into distinct temporal blocks representing economic regimes (e.g., pre- and post-2008); (2) vary the sampling frequency (monthly, quarterly) to test scale-dependence; and (3) conduct cross-economy validation (e.g., training on US data, testing on Eurozone data). Performance will be measured by size/power distortions and parameter stability across folds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, replicable framework for the empirical validation of cointegration methods, moving beyond illustrative examples. It will generate concrete guidance on the regimes and data structures where specific local-to-unity and functional coefficient models are most reliable, thereby enhancing their practical application in policy and forecasting settings.
proposals.novelty: The explicit translation of a robust validation paradigm from computational ecology to econometric theory is novel, as is the systematic application of a multi-regime, block-structured testing protocol to the evaluation of nonlinear cointegration and near-integrated processes.

proposals.title: A Test for the Null of No Cointegration Based on the Limit Distribution of the Akaike Information Criterion
proposals.motivation: Standard cointegration tests (e.g., Engle-Granger, Johansen) test the null hypothesis of no cointegration against the alternative of cointegration. However, the power and size properties of these tests can be sensitive to nuisance parameters and model specification. A novel approach that inverts a model selection criterion, rather than relying on a direct test statistic from a regression framework, could offer a robust alternative with different operating characteristics, particularly in finite samples or near-integrated environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymptotic distribution of the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) under the null of no cointegration (a system of independent unit root processes) is non-standard but pivotal, and its quantiles can be used to construct a valid and consistent test for the null hypothesis.
proposals.method: The research will first derive the limit distribution of the AIC (or its difference between the cointegrated and non-cointegrated model specifications) for a vector autoregression when the true data-generating process is a set of independent I(1) processes. This involves establishing weak convergence results for the relevant sample moments and the log-likelihood, extending Phillips's work on limit theory for near-integrated processes. The critical values will be simulated from this limit distribution. The test procedure will then be implemented by comparing the observed AIC value (or a transformed version) against the simulated critical values, rejecting the null of no cointegration when the AIC favors the cointegrated model sufficiently strongly.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, theoretically grounded test for cointegration that complements existing methods. It will expand the toolkit for empirical researchers, particularly in scenarios where model selection uncertainty is high. The project will also contribute to the theoretical understanding of information criteria in nonstationary time series models.
proposals.novelty: While AIC is universally used for lag selection and model choice, using its limit distribution to formally test a sharp null hypothesis of no cointegration is a novel inversion of its standard application. This approach connects model selection theory directly to hypothesis testing in a non-standard asymptotic framework.

proposals.title: Spectral Gap Determination of Cointegrating Rank: A Random Matrix Theory Approach
proposals.motivation: Determining the number of common stochastic trends (cointegrating rank) is a fundamental step in multivariate time series analysis, with existing methods like Johansen's trace test relying on sequential testing and asymptotic approximations that can be sensitive to lag specification and sample size. A robust, scaling-based alternative rooted in the intrinsic geometry of the data could offer improved finite-sample performance and a complementary perspective to likelihood-based inference.
proposals.hypothesis: The eigenvalues of a suitably normalized long-run covariance matrix of a multivariate time series will exhibit a spectral gap, and the number of eigenvalues lying outside the bulk distribution predicted by Random Matrix Theory (RMT) for pure noise corresponds to the rank of the common trend space.
proposals.method: We will construct a nonparametric, kernel-smoothed long-run covariance matrix from the first-differenced data. Its eigenvalues will be computed and compared to the theoretical limiting distribution under a null of no cointegration (e.g., a scaled Marchenko-Pastur law for serially dependent data or the Tracy-Widom law for the largest eigenvalue). The selection rule will identify the rank as the number of eigenvalues exceeding a threshold defined by the edge of the theoretical noise bulk, potentially refined via hypothesis testing on extreme eigenvalues. The method's properties will be analyzed using Phillips's established framework for near-integrated and cointegrated systems.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, nonparametric, and geometrically intuitive cointegrating rank selection procedure, complementing traditional likelihood-based tests. It will establish the theoretical link between the spectral properties of long-run covariance matrices in cointegrated systems and RMT limits, offering a new toolkit for diagnosing common trends.
proposals.novelty: While RMT is standard in signal processing and physics for dimensionality estimation, its application to cointegration rank selection is novel. The key innovation is adapting RMT null distributions for the specific covariance structures of multivariate time series with stochastic trends, moving beyond i.i.d. noise assumptions.

proposals.title: Optimal Inference in Reduced Rank Regression with General Error Processes: Parametric and Nonparametric Specifications
proposals.motivation: The Reduced Rank Regression (RRR) model, fundamental to cointegration analysis, typically relies on a semiparametric error structure, assuming i.i.d. or mixingale innovations. This is restrictive for modern time series applications involving heteroskedasticity, conditional heteroskedasticity (e.g., GARCH), or persistent volatility. A fully specified error process can lead to efficiency gains, while flexible nonparametric specifications can provide robustness, yet the asymptotic theory for RRR estimation and testing under such generalizations remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the error process within the RRR framework—either parametrically (e.g., as a tempered linear process or GARCH) or via a local nonparametric estimator—we can derive sharper limit theories, construct more powerful point-optimal tests for cointegration rank, and achieve efficiency gains in estimating the cointegrating space.
proposals.method: We will extend the functional local-to-unity and tempered process frameworks from Phillips's recent work. For parametric specifications, we will derive the limit distribution of the Gaussian quasi-likelihood estimator under misspecification-robust assumptions, adapting the "irrelevant instruments" approach to handle generated regressors from the error model. For nonparametric specifications, we will employ a local empirical likelihood method, conditioning on a nonparametric estimator of the error covariance kernel, and establish a new functional central limit theorem for the sample moment process. The convergence of eigenvalues of the associated random matrix will be analyzed to develop rank tests.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified asymptotic theory for RRR with general error dependencies, directly applicable to financial and macroeconomic data with stochastic volatility. It will yield new, more efficient estimators and a family of point-optimal cointegration tests with improved size and power properties. The results will bridge the gap between traditional semiparametric cointegration analysis and modern volatility modeling.
proposals.novelty: While error specification is well-studied in univariate models, a systematic treatment within the multivariate RRR/cointegration framework, combining parametric tempering, local-to-unity asymptotics, and nonparametric conditioning, is novel. This moves beyond the standard i.i.d. or mixingale assumptions that dominate the pre-2026 cointegration literature.

proposals.title: Joint Penalized Estimation of Factor Rank and Volatility Breaks in High-Dimensional Nonstationary Systems
proposals.motivation: High-dimensional time series analysis, common in macroeconomics and finance, relies on factor models to reduce dimensionality. However, standard factor estimation assumes homoskedastic idiosyncratic errors, which is unrealistic and leads to inconsistent rank selection and factor estimates if volatility regimes are present. Pre-2026 literature treats factor number determination and break-point detection as separate, sequential problems, creating fragility and unaccounted-for joint uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified penalized likelihood framework, simultaneously controlling factor loading sparsity and the number of variance shifts, will yield consistent, jointly estimated factor ranks and break dates, with valid inference on both, even when factors exhibit near-integrated or locally nonstationary behavior.
proposals.method: We will extend a Gaussian quasi-likelihood model for the observed panel, where the covariance matrix has a low-rank factor component and a diagonal idiosyncratic component subject to multiple structural breaks. A composite penalty function, combining a group-LASSO penalty on factor loadings (for rank selection) and a fused LASSO or group penalty on the log-variances (for break detection), will be imposed. Estimation will proceed via a tailored alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm. Asymptotic theory will be developed under Phillips's local-to-unity and near-integrated process frameworks, establishing joint consistency and limiting distributions.
proposals.contribution: This research provides the first coherent methodology for joint inference on factor rank and volatility regimes, eliminating the fragility of two-step procedures. It will deliver a computationally efficient estimator with a theoretically grounded measure of joint estimation uncertainty, directly applicable to high-dimensional nonstationary data with stochastic volatility.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fully joint modeling and penalized estimation framework, which explicitly accounts for the feedback between determining the low-rank common component and identifying breaks in the idiosyncratic volatility—a problem previously addressed only sequentially.

proposals.title: Robust Cointegration Rank Selection with Estimated Volatility Break Points
proposals.motivation: A large pre-2026 literature on cointegration analysis, including Phillips's own work on optimal estimation and testing, often assumes either constant volatility or known structural break dates in the error variance. In practice, volatility regimes are unknown and must be estimated, which introduces estimation uncertainty into the critical procedure of selecting the cointegration rank. Ignoring this uncertainty can lead to spurious inference on the number of long-run relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formally incorporating the estimation error of volatility break points into the asymptotic distribution of cointegration rank test statistics, we can develop a modified testing procedure that controls size and maintains power, even when break dates are unknown.
proposals.method: The approach builds on the framework of point-optimal tests for near-integrated processes and functional local asymptotics. First, we will derive the limiting distribution of the Gaussian quasi-likelihood ratio (QLR) statistic for cointegration rank under a local-to-unity setup, where the break date estimator converges at rate T. This involves extending Phillips's work on functionally local roots to a multivariate system with estimated variance regimes. We will then develop a modified test statistic, potentially via a subsampling or bootstrap procedure calibrated to this limit theory, to correct for the break-point estimation effect.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a theoretically grounded and practical method for determining cointegration rank in the presence of unmodeled volatility shifts. It will extend the applicability of cointegration analysis to macroeconomic and financial datasets with uncertain instability periods. The output will be a new set of critical values and an associated testing procedure for empirical researchers.
proposals.novelty: While break-point estimation and cointegration testing are mature fields, their intersection—specifically adjusting rank selection for the precision of estimated volatility breaks—is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in the functional local asymptotic treatment of the break-date estimator within the multivariate likelihood ratio framework.

proposals.title: Consistent Rank Selection in Cointegrated Systems with Long-Memory Disturbances: A Penalty Rescaling Framework
proposals.motivation: Information criteria like BIC are standard for selecting the cointegrating rank in vector error correction models (VECMs), but their consistency relies on short-memory, I(0) errors. In applied macroeconomics and finance, persistent errors with long-range dependence or anti-persistence are increasingly documented. Existing rank selection procedures fail under such conditions, leading to spurious inference on the number of stochastic trends, a fundamental parameter for policy and forecasting.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymptotic bias in standard penalty terms under long-memory errors can be characterized precisely and corrected through a memory-parameter-dependent rescaling, restoring consistency of the rank selection procedure across a wide class of error processes.
proposals.method: We first derive the limiting distribution of the log-likelihood ratio for rank tests under errors modeled as linear processes with memory parameter d ∈ (-0.5, 0.5), extending Phillips' work on near-integrated and tempered processes. This reveals the divergence rate of the information criterion penalty needed for consistency. The core procedure involves a two-step estimator: first, consistently estimate d from the residuals of a full-rank VECM using a local Whittle or similar semiparametric method; second, construct a rescaled penalty term, λ_T(d̂), for a modified criterion (e.g., BIC*(d̂)) that adapts to the estimated memory. The asymptotic theory will cover both Gaussian and non-Gaussian innovations.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first unified, theoretically justified framework for consistent cointegrating rank selection robust to error memory. It will deliver a practical, implementable algorithm for applied researchers working with persistent time series. The results will significantly enhance the reliability of cointegration analysis in fields like empirical macroeconomics and financial econometrics.
proposals.novelty: While memory estimation and rank selection are mature topics separately, their integration into a single adaptive criterion is novel. The explicit derivation of the penalty rescaling function from the non-standard limit theory of the likelihood under long-memory errors represents a significant theoretical advance beyond standard I(0)/I(1) asymptotics.

proposals.title: Consistent Rank Selection in Cointegrated Systems with Long Memory Errors: A Frequency-Domain Information Criterion
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature establishes the consistency of rank selection criteria (like BIC) for cointegrated VAR models under weak dependence assumptions. However, many economic and financial time series exhibit long memory or persistent noise, violating these assumptions and jeopardizing the consistency of conventional procedures. Extending these foundational results to accommodate such strong temporal dependence is crucial for reliable inference in applied macroeconomics and finance.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting model selection to the spectral domain using the Whittle likelihood approximation, we can construct a modified information criterion with a corrected penalty term that guarantees the consistent estimation of the cointegration rank even when errors follow a long-memory process.
proposals.method: We will derive a frequency-domain information criterion based on the tapered periodogram of the regression residuals. The criterion will approximate the likelihood using the Whittle function, explicitly incorporating the known or parametrically estimated spectral density of the long-memory error process (e.g., a fractional ARIMA). The key technical step is to rigorously analyze the asymptotic behavior of this criterion under local alternatives and near-integrated processes, extending Phillips's existing limit theory. The penalty term will be adjusted to account for the slow decay in the autocovariance function, ensuring model selection consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formally consistent procedure for selecting the cointegration rank in the presence of long-memory errors, closing a significant gap in the literature. It will deliver a practical, frequency-domain-adapted tool for applied researchers working with persistent data, enhancing the reliability of cointegration analysis in fields like econometrics and geophysics.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in econometrics, drawing a direct methodological analogy from geophysical signal processing to solve a long-standing problem in time series model selection. It non-trivially merges Phillips's limit theory for near-integrated processes with spectral-domain inference techniques tailored for long memory.

proposals.title: Limit Theory for Inconsistent Rank Selection Criteria under Stochastic Volatility: A Time-Changed Diffusion Approach
proposals.motivation: Inconsistent model selection criteria, such as certain forms of AIC or Mallows's Cp, remain widely used in applied time series econometrics despite their failure to select the true model asymptotically. Their finite-sample behavior, particularly under realistic heteroskedasticity like stochastic volatility, is poorly understood. Characterizing their limit distributions under such conditions is crucial for providing accurate inference and understanding the practical risks of their use.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymptotic distribution of an inconsistent selection criterion under time-varying variance can be characterized as a time-changed diffusion process, where the stochastic time change is driven by the cumulative integrated inverse variance, analogous to the treatment of neutral genetic drift in populations of fluctuating size.
proposals.method: We will model the sequence of criterion values (e.g., differences in log-likelihoods) as a martingale process. The key step is to apply a stochastic time change, transforming the process to a standard diffusion on a new clock defined by the cumulative quadratic variation, which is a function of the latent variance process. Using weak convergence arguments for near-integrated and locally explosive processes, we will derive the limiting stochastic differential equation. The analysis will employ functional limit theorems and local-to-unity asymptotics to handle the persistent components of the model selection problem.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general asymptotic theory for a class of inconsistent model selection procedures under heteroskedasticity, delivering non-Gaussian limit distributions essential for valid post-selection inference. It will bridge concepts from population genetics and econometric theory, offering a new technical framework for analyzing statistical procedures under stochastic scaling.
proposals.novelty: The novel application of the 'time-changed diffusion' concept from evolutionary biology to econometric model selection problems under stochastic volatility is unprecedented. It moves beyond standard homoskedastic asymptotic analyses to a more realistic, and analytically distinct, setting.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Distributions of Inconsistent Rank Selection Criteria Under Time-Varying Volatility
proposals.motivation: In cointegrated VAR analysis, standard information criteria like BIC are known to be consistent for rank selection under homoskedasticity. However, recent pre-2026 work has shown that these criteria can become inconsistent, leading to overestimation of cointegrating rank, in the presence of non-stationary or time-varying volatility. The asymptotic behavior of other popular but potentially inconsistent criteria, such as AIC or the Hannan-Quinn criterion, under such conditions remains uncharacterized, creating a gap in the theory for robust model selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under locally explosive or near-integrated volatility processes, rank selection criteria with weaker penalty terms (like AIC) will converge to non-degenerate limit distributions, rather than diverging, and that these distributions depend functionally on the path of the volatility process.
proposals.method: The approach builds on Phillips's recent work on near-integrated and tempered processes. We will model the error variance as a non-stationary process, such as a stochastic volatility model or a deterministic break function, embedded in a cointegrated VAR. Using functional limit theory and continuous mapping, we will derive the asymptotic distributions of the criteria by analyzing the limiting behavior of their penalty-adjusted likelihood ratios. The analysis will employ local-to-unity and near-integrated representations for the volatility process to characterize the non-standard limits.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first complete asymptotic theory for a class of inconsistent rank selection criteria under general time-varying variance, quantifying their bias. It will yield practical guidance on the reliability of different criteria in financial and macroeconomic applications where volatility clustering is prevalent, and may inform the development of robustified selection procedures.
proposals.novelty: While inconsistency of BIC under heteroskedasticity is known, deriving the explicit functional form of the limit distribution for AIC-type criteria under non-stationary volatility is novel. The extension of tempered process and local-to-unity limit theory to the domain of information criterion asymptotics represents a significant theoretical advance.

proposals.title: Unified MLE and Inference for Self-Exciting Threshold Autoregressions with Joint Mean and Volatility Regimes
proposals.motivation: Empirical time series, especially in finance and macroeconomics, exhibit nonlinear dynamics where shifts in persistence and volatility often coincide, as seen in business cycle phases or financial crises. Existing TAR and TARCH models typically treat mean and variance regimes separately, leading to sequential testing problems and potential model misspecification. A unified framework for joint inference is needed to correctly identify whether regimes are driven by mean shifts, volatility shifts, or both, which is crucial for accurate forecasting and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified maximum likelihood framework for SETAR models with concurrent threshold effects in the conditional mean and variance can provide consistent estimation and powerful, non-sequential tests to correctly distinguish between pure mean, pure volatility, and coupled regimes, leading to more parsimonious and interpretable models.
proposals.method: The approach develops a joint likelihood function for a SETAR model where both the autoregressive parameters and the innovation variance are regime-dependent, switching on a common lagged threshold variable. We employ a concentrated likelihood and grid search over the threshold parameter, extending the local-to-unity and near-integrated process asymptotic theory from Phillips's recent work to derive the limiting distribution of the threshold estimator and the sup-LR test for regime existence. A novel model selection criterion, penalizing both lags and thresholds across equations, will be constructed to ensure parsimony and guard against overfitting.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a statistically coherent method for the joint specification of nonlinear mean and volatility dynamics, resolving the sequential testing dilemma. It delivers robust inference on regime-specific persistence and volatility clustering, with direct applications to modeling financial volatility and macroeconomic instability. The theoretical results on the limiting behavior of estimators under local alternatives will extend the nonlinear unit root literature.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's core novelty is the fully joint treatment and testing of mean and volatility thresholds within a single likelihood framework, avoiding the pre-testing bias of two-step approaches. The integrated model selection criterion that simultaneously determines lag order and threshold existence for both equations is also a new contribution to the TAR literature.

proposals.title: Nonparametric Cointegrating Regression with Endogenous Regressors: An Instrumental Variable Framework and Limit Theory
proposals.motivation: Nonparametric cointegrating regression models are crucial for capturing flexible long-run relationships in economics and finance, but existing asymptotic theory typically assumes strict exogeneity of the regressors. This is a severe limitation in practice, where simultaneity and omitted variables often induce endogeneity, leading to inconsistent estimation and invalid inference. Addressing this gap is essential for robust empirical analysis of nonlinear cointegrating relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing a valid, locally stationary instrumental variable within a nonparametric kernel framework, it is possible to obtain a consistent estimator for a nonlinear cointegrating regression with endogenous regressors and derive its asymptotic distribution as a mixed Gaussian process.
proposals.method: The approach extends Phillips's work on local limit theory and irrelevant instruments. We will employ a nonparametric IV estimator using kernel smoothing, where the instrument is assumed to be 'locally exogenous' in the sense of being cointegrated with the endogenous regressor but uncorrelated with the error at high frequencies. The asymptotic theory will treat the regressor as a near-integrated process, building on recent work on tempered linear and functionally local-to-unity roots. The limit distribution will be derived by establishing a new functional limit theorem for the product of the kernel-weighted instrument and the error process, leveraging martingale approximation techniques.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comprehensive limit theory for nonparametric cointegrating regression under endogeneity, delivering a practical estimation and testing framework. It will extend the toolkit for analyzing nonlinear long-run equilibria in macroeconomics and asset pricing where strict exogeneity fails, bridging the gap between nonparametric methods and instrumental variable econometrics for persistent data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of nonparametric cointegration theory with an IV framework for persistent data, moving beyond the exogenous regressor assumption. The derivation of the limit theory for a kernel-based IV estimator under near-integrated regressors and instruments represents a significant and non-trivial advancement over existing point-optimal and local-to-unity testing literature.

proposals.title: Testing for Dynamic Misspecification in Multivariate Nonlinear Cointegration Systems
proposals.motivation: Empirical work in macroeconomics and finance increasingly posits multiple long-run equilibrium relationships that are nonlinear, such as threshold or smooth transition cointegration. However, existing asymptotic theory and testing procedures, like those for linear cointegration or univariate nonlinear models, are inadequate for these multivariate nonlinear systems. A key, unresolved pre-2026 challenge is distinguishing genuine nonlinear cointegration from dynamic misspecification (e.g., omitted variables, parameter instability) within a system of equations, as both can produce similar spurious nonlinearities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the concept of functional local-to-unity roots and tempered processes to a multivariate nonlinear framework, we can develop Lagrange Multiplier and point-optimal tests that consistently identify dynamic misspecification even when multiple, potentially misspecified, nonlinear cointegrating relations are present.
proposals.method: The approach builds on Phillips's recent work on functional local-to-unity roots and tempered processes. First, we specify a system of nonlinear cointegrating regressions with error correction terms following a multivariate tempered linear process. Second, we derive the limiting distributions of parameter estimates under local alternatives where the nonlinear functional form is misspecified. Third, we construct a suite of tests: (i) a system-wide LM test for residual-based nonlinearity against a locally misspecified alternative, and (ii) point-optimal invariant tests for the presence of 'tempered' versus 'near-integrated' components in the system's error dynamics, which serve as indicators of misspecification.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first formal asymptotic theory for testing dynamic misspecification in systems with multiple nonlinear cointegrating relationships. It will deliver practical, optimal testing procedures that allow applied researchers to validate the functional form of multivariate nonlinear equilibrium models, preventing spurious inference. The work will directly extend the limit theory for near-integrated and tempered processes into a multivariate nonlinear cointegration context.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the multivariate cointegration systems framework with the recent asymptotic theory for functionally local-to-unity and tempered processes to address the model validation problem for *systems* of nonlinear relationships, a significant gap beyond the current univariate or linear system focus.

proposals.title: Testing for Cointegration with Time-Varying Coefficients in the Presence of Dynamic Misspecification
proposals.motivation: Empirical cointegrating relationships in economics and finance, such as long-run money demand or purchasing power parity, are often subject to structural breaks. Standard cointegration tests and estimators assume a fixed long-run parameter, leading to size distortions and loss of power when breaks occur. Furthermore, dynamic misspecification in the short-run adjustment process can confound inference on the long-run relationship, a problem not yet fully analyzed in models with time-varying cointegrating vectors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the asymptotic distributions of standard cointegration tests (e.g., residual-based and system tests) are non-pivotal under the joint presence of structural breaks in the cointegrating vector and local-to-unity dynamics or other forms of dynamic misspecification. A new class of point-optimal invariant tests can be constructed to control size and improve power in this setting.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of 'near integrated' and 'functionally local to unity' processes to allow the long-run parameter to follow a broken or smooth transition function.
