The method involves: 1) Deriving the limiting distribution of the likelihood ratio statistic under the null of no cointegration, allowing for a time-varying candidate cointegrating vector. 2) Employing a modified version of the Phillips-Hansen fully modified OLS (FM-OLS) and canonical cointegrating regression (CCR) to estimate break points or functional forms, building on Bai-Perron and Park-Hahn procedures. 3) Developing a point-optimal invariant test, where the point of optimization accounts for the break structure, following the principles in Phillips's work on optimal testing with local alternatives.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a robust testing procedure for cointegration that is valid when the long-run equilibrium relationship evolves over time. It will precisely characterize the interaction between break-induced nonstationarity and near-integrated dynamics, filling a gap between the structural break and local-to-unity literatures. The resulting tests will be directly applicable to macroeconomic and financial datasets where regime changes are suspected.
proposals.novelty: While breaks in cointegration are studied, and dynamic misspecification is studied, their joint effect on inference is not well understood. The novelty lies in integrating Phillips's recent work on functional local-to-unity processes and point-optimal testing into a model with a time-varying cointegrating space, creating a unified limit theory for this prevalent empirical problem.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Properties of Local Polynomial Regression under Dynamic Misspecification and Near-Integrated Regressors
proposals.motivation: Nonparametric estimators like local polynomial regression are widely used for their flexibility, but their asymptotic theory often assumes stationary or strictly exogenous regressors. In econometrics, many variables exhibit persistent, near-unit-root dynamics. The impact of such dynamic misspecification—where the functional form is correctly modeled but the regressor's stochastic properties are mischaracterized—on these estimators' limit distributions is not fully understood, posing risks to inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when local polynomial regression is applied to data generated by a cointegrating or near-integrated system, the estimator's convergence rate and limiting distribution are fundamentally altered, leading to non-standard asymptotics that depend on the local-to-unity parameter and the bandwidth selection.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of Phillips (J. Econometrics, 2015) for nonlinear cointegration to local polynomial regression. The approach involves: 1) Setting up a data-generating process with a near-integrated regressor and a nonparametric conditional mean. 2) Deriving the asymptotic distribution of the local polynomial estimator by employing a functional limit theory for partial sums of kernel-weighted score processes. 3) Conducting a detailed analysis of the bias-variance trade-off under misspecified dynamics, focusing on how optimal bandwidth rates change. 4) Developing point-optimal testing procedures for the significance of the nonparametric function in this context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous limit theory for a major class of nonparametric estimators under realistic persistent data conditions, bridging the gap between nonparametric econometrics and unit root asymptotics. It will yield practical guidance on bandwidth choice and valid inference procedures for applied researchers working with persistent time series.
proposals.novelty: While local polynomial asymptotics are well-known for stationary data, and Phillips has analyzed nonlinear cointegration, the explicit fusion of these areas to derive the precise non-standard limit behavior of local polynomials under near-integration is novel and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Limit Theory for Semiparametric Index Models with Near-Integrated Regressors
proposals.motivation: Semiparametric index models, such as single-index and partially linear models, are widely used in econometrics for their flexibility. However, their asymptotic theory is predominantly developed under strict stationarity assumptions. In practice, many economic and financial time series exhibit near-unit root or local-to-unity persistence, a feature for which the limit theory of these models remains underdeveloped. This gap limits reliable inference in applications involving trending or highly persistent data.
proposals.hypothesis: The limit distributions for estimators and test statistics in semiparametric index models, when regressors are near-integrated, are non-standard and can be characterized as functionals of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes and local time. These distributions differ fundamentally from the standard normal or chi-square limits obtained under stationarity.
proposals.method: We will extend the recent limit theory for near-integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes to the semiparametric context. The core technical approach involves deriving the asymptotic behavior of the sample moment conditions that define the semiparametric estimators (e.g., the average derivative estimator for single-index models) when the data generating process follows a local-to-unity specification. This will employ functional limit theory and continuous mapping arguments. We will then develop point-optimal testing procedures, adapting the framework from Phillips's recent work, to test hypotheses on the index parameters in this non-standard setting.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first unified limit theory for a class of semiparametric models under near-integration, enabling valid inference in a wide range of applied settings with persistent data. It will yield new, robust testing procedures with known asymptotic properties, directly addressing a significant practical limitation in the current toolkit for nonlinear time series analysis.
proposals.novelty: While the limit theory for parametric models with nonstationary data is mature, its application to semiparametric index models is novel. The key innovation is the synthesis of Phillips's recent work on local-to-unity limit theory with the kernel-based estimation framework of semiparametrics, a synthesis not previously attempted in the literature.

proposals.title: Testing for Dynamic Misspecification in Nonlinear Cointegrating Panel Data Models with Local-to-Unity Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The application of nonlinear cointegration models to panel data has grown, driven by empirical needs in macroeconomics and finance. However, existing panel estimators often assume correct dynamic specification, which is rarely justified in practice. Misspecified short-run dynamics can lead to inconsistent estimates of the nonlinear long-run relationship and invalid inference, a problem not yet systematically addressed for panels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard panel estimators for nonlinear cointegration (e.g., pooled or mean-group) are inconsistent under dynamic misspecification, but that a suite of point-optimal tests, adapted to the panel setting, can reliably detect such misspecification when the underlying processes exhibit near-integrated (local-to-unity) behavior.
proposals.method: The approach extends Phillips's recent work on point-optimal testing and near-integrated processes to a heterogeneous panel. We will first derive the asymptotic local power functions for a panel version of the test under the sequence of local alternatives. The method involves constructing a panel Lagrange Multiplier (LM) or Wald statistic based on the score from a local linear approximation of the nonlinear cointegrating function. The limiting distribution will be characterized as a functional of Brownian motions, with critical values obtained via simulation. The finite-sample performance will be evaluated using Monte Carlo experiments with various nonlinear functions (e.g., logistic, exponential) and cross-sectional dependence structures.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first formal framework for testing dynamic specification in nonlinear cointegrating panels, filling a critical gap in the applied literature. It will deliver practical, robust testing procedures that safeguard against spurious inference in empirical panel studies involving persistent data and nonlinear equilibria.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the theory of point-optimal testing for local-to-unity roots with the challenges of panel data nonlinear cointegration, creating a test specifically for dynamic misspecification—a problem distinct from and more subtle than simple unit root or cointegration testing in panels.

proposals.title: Optimal Inference in Cointegrated Systems with Near-Integrated and Functionally Local-to-Unity Regressors
proposals.motivation: Empirical work in macroeconomics and finance frequently encounters persistent time series that are not pure unit roots but exhibit near-integrated or local-to-unity behavior. The asymptotic theory for cointegration and instrumental variables (IV) estimation, as in Phillips's recent work on irrelevant instruments, is typically developed for I(1) and I(0) processes. This creates a gap between the standard limit theory and the finite-sample reality of highly persistent, yet not perfectly integrated, data-generating processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the framework of cointegrated systems with possibly irrelevant instruments to regressors with roots that are functionally local to unity will yield a more accurate and powerful asymptotic theory. This theory will show that optimal estimators and tests require non-standard local approximations that differ from the pure I(1) case.
proposals.method: We will employ a functional local-to-unity framework, modeling the autoregressive root as ρ_T = 1 + c/T + g(T)/T, where g(T) is a slowly varying function, extending recent work on point optimal testing. The analysis will use weak convergence theory for sample covariances of such near-integrated processes and Ornstein-Uhlenbeck limit processes. We will derive the limit distributions of the IV estimator and associated Wald statistics, then apply the limits-of-experiments approach to characterize and construct point-optimal invariant tests for cointegration rank in this generalized setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified asymptotic theory for inference in cointegrated systems under more realistic, persistent regressor assumptions. It will deliver modified critical values and new point-optimal test procedures that are robust to the degree of near-integration, thereby improving the reliability of empirical conclusions in applied work.
proposals.novelty: While local-to-unity theory exists for univariate and some multivariate problems, its integration into the framework of cointegrated systems with instrument relevance considerations is novel. The key innovation is the functional local-to-unity specification within the simultaneous equations context, which generalizes the standard 'c/T' local alternative.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Theory for Nonlinear Regressions with Deterministically Trending Near-Integrated Regressors
proposals.motivation: Empirical work in macroeconomics and finance frequently involves regressors with persistent stochastic trends and deterministic time trends (e.g., output, consumption). Existing asymptotic theory for nonlinear regressions with near-integrated processes, a key area of Phillips's recent work, typically assumes zero mean or simple demeaning. Ignoring a deterministic trend component before applying a nonlinear transformation can lead to severe misspecification and invalid inference on the nonlinear relationship.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a deterministic trend component into the data-generating process of a near-integrated regressor prior to a nonlinear transformation will fundamentally alter the limiting distributions of the regression estimates and associated test statistics, requiring new functional approximations and local-to-unity limit theory.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of 'near integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes' to include a polynomial deterministic trend. The core technical approach involves developing a new functional limit theory for the partial sum process of the detrended series. We will employ continuous mapping theorems to derive the asymptotic distributions of nonlinear least squares estimators and associated t-statistics, building on the 'point optimal testing' and 'local to unity' methodologies. The analysis will specifically characterize the interplay between the localizing parameter for the root, the trend parameters, and the shape of the nonlinear function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous asymptotic foundation for applied researchers using nonlinear models with trending persistent data. It will deliver new, robust point-optimal tests for the presence of a unit root in the presence of deterministic trends and nonlinearity. The results will directly extend the toolkit for 'optimal estimation of cointegrated systems' to more realistic, trend-inclusive data environments.
proposals.novelty: While detrending in linear unit root models is standard, the joint treatment of deterministic trends and nonlinear transformations within the local-to-unity asymptotic framework is novel. The non-commutativity of detrending and nonlinear transformation creates a distinct theoretical problem not addressed in prior limit theory for nonlinear regressions with persistent regressors.

proposals.title: Inference on the Tempering Parameter in Near-Integrated Processes: Point-Optimal Tests and Local-to-Unity Asymptotics
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Phillips (2024) introduces tempered linear processes, where a sample-size-dependent tempering parameter (κ_n) governs the transition from a unit root to a stationary process. This framework is crucial for modeling persistent but non-explosive economic and financial time series. However, no formal inference procedures exist for κ_n, leaving a critical gap in the practical application of these models for distinguishing between different degrees of near-integratedness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a point-optimal invariant testing framework, combined with a local-to-unity asymptotic analysis where κ_n → 0 as n → ∞, can yield tractable and powerful inference procedures for the tempering parameter. The limiting distributions of the test statistics will be non-standard but pivotal under the null.
proposals.method: The approach builds on Phillips's recent work on point-optimal testing with functionally local roots. We will derive the Gaussian likelihood ratio statistic for testing a simple null on κ_n, accounting for the presence of an unknown localizing coefficient (c). The method involves embedding κ_n within the local-to-unity framework as κ_n = g(c)/n^δ, developing the limit theory for the resulting sample moment processes under this double asymptotic. We will then tabulate critical values via simulation of the limiting functionals of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes and assess power against relevant local alternatives.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first dedicated estimation and testing toolkit for the tempering parameter, enabling empirical researchers to formally assess the degree of tempering in near-integrated series. It will extend the theoretical frontier of limit theory for non-standard, nonlinear time series models and deliver practical test statistics with known asymptotic properties.
proposals.novelty: While point-optimal tests exist for the local-to-unity parameter, applying this framework to the tempering parameter—which governs the *functional form* of the root's decay—is novel. The key innovation is the joint asymptotic treatment of the localizing coefficient (c) and the vanishing tempering rate (κ_n), a problem not addressed in prior literature on near-integrated or tempered processes.

proposals.title: Cointegration Analysis with Tempered Equilibrium Errors: Theory, Inference, and Optimal Testing
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Phillips (2024) and others has developed a limit theory for near-integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes, which exhibit a smooth transition from I(1)-like to I(0)-like behavior. This provides a more realistic model for many economic and financial time series where shocks have persistent but ultimately transient effects. However, the theory for cointegrating relationships—the cornerstone of equilibrium modeling—where the equilibrium error follows such a tempered process remains undeveloped, limiting empirical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the asymptotic distributions of standard cointegration estimators (like FM-OLS) and test statistics (like the Engle-Granger ADF test) will be fundamentally altered when equilibrium errors are tempered, leading to non-standard local power functions and requiring new optimal inference procedures tailored to the tempering parameter.
proposals.method: We will extend the theoretical framework of 'near integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes' to a bivariate/system context. The core technical approach involves deriving the joint asymptotic distribution of the sample moments for a cointegrated system where the equilibrium error is a tempered linear process. This will utilize local-to-unity and functional limit theory for tempered processes. We will then develop point-optimal invariant tests for the null of no cointegration against the tempered local alternative, building on Phillips's recent work on point optimal testing with functionally local roots. The finite-sample performance will be evaluated via Monte Carlo simulations.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first complete asymptotic theory for cointegration under tempered errors, delivering new limiting distributions for estimators and tests. It will yield a new class of point-optimal cointegration tests with superior power against empirically relevant alternatives where equilibrium corrections are persistent but not permanent. This bridges the gap between the new tempered process literature and empirical cointegration analysis.
proposals.novelty: While tempering has been studied for univariate and near-integrated processes, its application and implications for cointegrated systems—where the focus is on the equilibrium *relationship*—are entirely novel. The development of optimal tests specifically against tempered alternatives is a non-obvious and significant advance beyond standard cointegration testing.

proposals.title: Optimal Inference for Cointegrating Regressions with Deterministic Trend Components
proposals.motivation: Empirical work in macroeconomics and finance frequently involves trending nonstationary data, where the long-run equilibrium relationship may itself evolve linearly or polynomially over time. Pre-2026 asymptotic theory for cointegrated systems, including Phillips's work on near-integrated processes and optimal estimation, often assumes a constant cointegrating vector or treats deterministic trends via demeaning/detrending prior to analysis. A unified framework for estimation and inference that explicitly incorporates parametric trends within the cointegrating regression function is lacking but essential for accurate modeling of structural relations with secular drift.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymptotic distributions of estimators and test statistics in cointegrated systems are fundamentally altered when deterministic trends are parameters of the cointegrating relation itself, and new point-optimal tests and estimators can be developed that dominate conventional two-step methods in terms of local power and efficiency.
proposals.method: We extend the functional local-to-unity and tempered process frameworks to regressions of the form y_t = μ + δ t + β' x_t + u_t, where x_t is a vector of near-integrated processes. The method involves deriving the limiting distributions of the trend (δ) and cointegration (β) parameters using triangular array asymptotics and continuous mapping onto Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes with drift. We will develop a point-optimal invariant test for the cointegration rank in the presence of a trend component, building on the Lagrangian and GLS approaches in Phillips's recent work on optimal estimation with irrelevant instruments.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a complete asymptotic theory for cointegrating regressions with endogenous trend components, delivering new efficient estimators and powerful hypothesis tests. The results will directly inform applied researchers on correctly specifying and testing for cointegration in the presence of deterministic evolutionary forces within the equilibrium relationship.
proposals.novelty: While detrending is standard, modeling the trend as a parameter within the cointegrating equation and deriving optimal procedures for its inference under local-to-unity regressors is a novel integration of Phillips's recent limit theory for nonlinear, near-integrated systems with classic cointegration regression.

proposals.title: Inference in Nonstationary Regression with Tempered and Locally Explosive Regressors: A Non-Gaussian Limit Theory
proposals.motivation: The asymptotic theory for nonstationary time series regression, pioneered by Phillips, heavily relies on weak convergence to functionals of standard Brownian motion. However, many empirically relevant processes, such as tempered unit roots, locally explosive processes, or processes driven by innovations with infinite variance, yield limiting distributions that are non-Gaussian. Pre-2026 work on 'tempered' and 'functionally local to unity' processes has begun to explore these boundaries, but a unified limit theory for inference when the regressor's limit is a general, non-Gaussian process remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by characterizing the class of non-Gaussian limiting processes arising from tempered, locally explosive, or heavy-tailed innovations, we can derive a general asymptotic distribution theory for least squares and instrumental variable estimators, leading to robust inference procedures that are pivotal under this broader class.
proposals.method: The approach extends the functional limit theory framework of Phillips (1987) and subsequent work on near-integrated processes. We will first model the regressor as a linear process with tempered or locally explosive autoregressive roots, or with innovations in the domain of attraction of a stable law. Using weak convergence arguments in Skorokhod space, we will derive the joint limit of the sample moments, which will involve functionals of non-Gaussian processes like tempered stable processes or Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes driven by Lévy motions. Point-optimal and locally best invariant test principles will then be applied to construct test statistics whose limiting distributions are simulated via direct numerical methods.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a comprehensive limit theory for regression with a non-Gaussian regressor limit, directly extending the classical Dickey-Fuller and Phillips-Perron paradigms. It will yield new, robust critical values and power envelopes for unit root and cointegration tests in models where the driving shocks exhibit tempering, local explosivity, or heavy tails, significantly broadening the applicability of nonstationary econometrics.
proposals.novelty: While previous work treats non-Gaussian limits (e.g., infinite variance) or non-standard drifts (e.g., tempering) in isolation, this proposal's novelty lies in developing a unified inference framework that systematically handles the convergence to a general class of non-Gaussian processes, explicitly linking the model's functional local parameter to the resulting non-standard limit law.

proposals.title: Nonparametric Cointegrating Regression with Multiple Integrated Regressors: Limit Theory and Inference
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on nonparametric structural models and cointegrating regression has primarily focused on the bivariate case with a single integrated regressor. Many empirical applications in economics and finance, however, involve relationships where a stationary variable depends on several nonstationary, potentially cointegrated, explanatory factors. The asymptotic theory and inferential procedures for such multivariate nonparametric settings are underdeveloped, limiting the practical application of flexible functional form models to systems with persistent data.
proposals.hypothesis: The limit distribution of a kernel-smoothed nonparametric estimator in a cointegrating regression with multiple integrated regressors can be characterized as a function of a multivariate Brownian motion process, and pivotal test statistics for the unknown functional relationship can be constructed using a fully modified or leads-and-lags correction to account for endogenous regressors and serial correlation.
proposals.method: The approach extends the framework of Phillips (2009, ET) and Wang & Phillips (2009, 2016) to a multivariate I(1) regressor setting. We will first derive the asymptotic limit of the Nadaraya-Watson estimator using local time theory and multivariate Brownian motion, establishing the mixed normal distribution and convergence rates. The core technical challenge involves handling the cointegration and potential collinearity among the regressors. Inference will be based on a fully modified nonparametric regression (FM-NPR) technique, adapting the parametric FM-OLS principle to estimate and subtract off the second-order bias terms in the kernel estimator's asymptotic distribution, enabling standard normal inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete limit theory for nonparametric cointegrating regression with multiple integrated regressors, filling a significant gap in the literature. It will deliver a practical, asymptotically valid inference procedure, enabling applied researchers to model complex, nonlinear long-run relationships without imposing restrictive parametric functional forms in multivariate nonstationary systems.
proposals.novelty: While multivariate cointegration in parametric systems is well-understood, the extension to a fully nonparametric structural model with multiple stochastic trends is novel. The key innovation lies in managing the interaction of multiple local times and developing a feasible fully modified correction in a nonparametric kernel-based framework, which is not a straightforward generalization of the univariate case.

proposals.title: Limit Theory and Inference for Regressions with Stationary but Highly Persistent Regressors
proposals.motivation: Much of the asymptotic theory for time series regression, including Phillips's seminal work, focuses on the dichotomy between stationary (I(0)) and unit root (I(1)) processes. However, in finite samples, highly persistent stationary autoregressive processes with roots near, but strictly inside, the unit circle blur this distinction. Standard inference, based on conventional stationary or nonstationary asymptotics, can be severely misleading in this empirically relevant 'local-to-unity' stationary setting, which lacks a unified theoretical framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the regressor as a stationary AR(1) process with a local-to-unity root of the form ρ = 1 - c/T^γ for 0 < γ ≤ 1, a new class of limiting distributions will emerge for regression coefficients and associated test statistics. These limits will bridge the gap between standard stationary (γ=0) and near-integrated (γ=1) asymptotics.
proposals.method: The approach employs functional limit theory and weak convergence to Brownian motion. We will derive the asymptotic distribution of the ordinary least squares estimator in a simple regression model y_t = β x_{t-1} + u_t, where x_t = ρ x_{t-1} + ε_t, with ρ local-to-unity as defined. The analysis will involve characterizing the limiting behavior of sample moments like T^{-(1+γ)/2} ∑ x_{t-1} u_t and T^{-(1+γ)} ∑ x_{t-1}^2, using a tailored normalization. Point-optimal and locally best invariant test statistics for H0: β=0 will be constructed based on this limit theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified limit theory for regression with stationary but highly persistent regressors, filling a gap between the I(0) and I(1) paradigms. It will deliver formally justified critical values and power functions for hypothesis testing in this prevalent but poorly understood context, directly impacting empirical work in macroeconomics and finance where persistent stationary series are common.
proposals.novelty: While local-to-unity theory exists for unit root testing and cointegration, its application to derive inference for a *stationary* regressor with a locally drifting autoregressive root is novel. This extends Phillips's work on 'functionally local to unity' roots and optimal testing into the core domain of stationary regression analysis.

proposals.title: Optimal Inference in Cointegrating Regressions with Endogenous Regressors and Local-to-Unity Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Phillips's recent work on optimal estimation and testing in cointegrated systems with irrelevant instruments and near-integrated processes assumes exogeneity of the regressors. In empirical macroeconomics and finance, this assumption is frequently violated, as regressors like interest rates or price indices are often contemporaneously correlated with structural shocks. This endogeneity invalidates standard inference, leading to biased estimates and unreliable tests, a critical gap in the pre-2026 literature on functional local-to-unity models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modified instrumental variables (IV) framework, using instruments that are functionally local to unity, can provide consistent estimation and point-optimal tests for cointegrating vectors even when the primary regressor is endogenous and exhibits near-integrated behavior.
proposals.method: The approach extends the 'irrelevant instruments' framework of Phillips (2025) to the endogenous case. We will model the endogenous regressor and the instrument as co-dependent tempered linear processes with local-to-unity roots. The core technical task is to derive the joint limiting distribution of the IV estimator and associated test statistics (e.g., Wald, LM) under this data-generating process. This involves developing new functional limit theorems for the sample moment conditions and establishing the asymptotic properties of a point-optimal test for the cointegrating coefficient, building on the local-to-unity testing literature.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comprehensive asymptotic theory for optimal IV estimation and testing in cointegrated systems where both the regressor and the instrument are near-integrated. It will deliver a practical, theoretically grounded testing procedure robust to endogeneity for applied researchers working with persistent economic data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous treatment of endogeneity and local-to-unity dynamics within an optimal inference framework, moving beyond the exogenous regressor assumption that underpins most pre-2026 limit theory for cointegration.

proposals.title: Inference in Near-Cointegrated Systems with Weakly Dependent Innovations: A Functional Local-to-Unity Framework
proposals.motivation: Empirical cointegration analysis often assumes either exact cointegration or a simple local-to-unity model for the error correction term, typically with i.i.d. innovations. This ignores the prevalent weak dependence (e.g., short memory, mixing) in economic and financial time series. The joint effect of near-cointegration (a root functionally local to unity) and general weak dependence on estimation and testing remains poorly understood, leading to potential dynamic misspecification and unreliable inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the asymptotic distributions of estimators and test statistics in near-cointegrated systems are fundamentally altered by the presence of weakly dependent innovations, requiring a new limit theory that jointly models the local-to-unity parameter and the dependence structure of the driving errors.
proposals.method: We will extend Phillips's recent work on functional local-to-unity processes and tempered linear processes. The approach involves: 1) Specifying a DGP where the error correction term follows a near-integrated process driven by a linear process with absolutely summable coefficients, nesting ARMA and other short-memory structures. 2) Deriving new functional central limit theorems and weak convergence results for sample moments and the OLS estimator using a double-array asymptotic framework where the localizing parameter and sample size evolve together. 3) Developing point-optimal invariant tests for the null of no cointegration against the alternative of near-cointegration in this setting, using modified versions of the Phillips-Ouliaris tests.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified asymptotic theory for near-cointegrated systems with general weakly dependent errors, correcting the size distortions of existing tests. It will yield new, robust critical values and establish the local power properties of cointegration tests under more realistic data-generating processes, directly enhancing empirical practice in macroeconometrics and financial econometrics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the joint asymptotic analysis of two distinct sources of persistence: the functional local-to-unity root and the weak dependence in the innovations, moving beyond the standard i.i.d. or simple martingale difference assumptions that dominate the pre-2026 cointegration literature.

proposals.title: Bias-Corrected Estimation in Panel Regressions with Cross-Sectional Dependence: A Functional Local Limit Theory Approach
proposals.motivation: Panel data models with cross-sectional dependence are ubiquitous in economics and finance, but standard estimators suffer from significant bias when the dependence structure is misspecified or unmodeled. Pre-2026 literature, including work on factor models and spatial correlation, shows that this bias persists asymptotically, leading to inconsistent inference and distorted test sizes. This problem is acute in near-integrated or cointegrated panel settings where the signal is weak and the limit distributions are non-standard.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by deriving the exact form of the random limit bias induced by general forms of cross-sectional dependence, we can construct analytical and simulation-based bias corrections that yield median-unbiased estimators and pivotal limit distributions for inference.
proposals.method: The approach extends Phillips's recent work on functional local-to-unity asymptotics and optimal testing. First, we model the error process as a tempered linear process with a factor or spatial dependence structure, deriving the joint limit theory for the panel estimator as both time series and cross-sectional dimensions grow. Second, we characterize the random bias component in this limit. Third, we develop a two-step estimator: (1) a preliminary consistent estimate of the dependence structure (e.g., via principal components or quasi-maximum likelihood), followed by (2) an analytical or recursive bootstrap bias correction targeting the median of the derived non-standard limit distribution.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified framework for bias-corrected estimation in panels with cross-sectional dependence, applicable to both stationary and non-stationary data. It will deliver new, feasible estimators with improved finite-sample properties and reliable inference procedures, directly advancing empirical work in macroeconomics and financial econometrics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the literature on cross-sectional dependence with the precise limit theory for near-integrated processes, explicitly solving for the bias in this non-standard setting. Unlike prior corrections for independent panels, our method directly addresses the randomness of the bias limit induced by dependence.

proposals.title: Finite Sample Bias in Dynamic Regressions with Non-Stationary Predetermined Regressors: Theory and Optimal Point Tests
proposals.motivation: The finite sample bias of OLS in autoregressive models with stationary predetermined regressors is well-understood. However, many macroeconomic and financial applications involve regressors that are non-stationary (e.g., near-integrated) and predetermined, not strictly exogenous. The bias properties and optimal inference in this prevalent but analytically challenging setting remain largely unexplored, posing risks to empirical conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the finite sample bias in models with non-stationary predetermined regressors follows a distinct functional form, characterized by a nonlinear function of the localizing parameter, and that point-optimal tests can be constructed to account for this bias structure to improve inference.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of Phillips (2014, ET) on near-integrated processes driven by tempered linear processes. The analysis will employ local-to-unity asymptotics and functional limit theory to derive the asymptotic distribution of the OLS estimator. The bias function will be characterized via stochastic integral representations. Building on the point optimal testing approach of Phillips (2018, JOE), we will develop a locally best invariant test statistic that is a nonlinear function of the data, optimized against the specific bias profile induced by the non-stationary predetermined regressor.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal characterization of finite sample bias in a general class of models with non-stationary predetermined regressors. It will deliver a practical, point-optimal testing procedure that controls size and improves power for inference on the autoregressive parameter in such models, directly benefiting applied time series econometrics.
proposals.novelty: The extension of bias analysis to the non-stationary predetermined case is novel, as existing literature focuses on stationary or strictly exogenous non-stationary regressors. Combining this bias analysis with the construction of point-optimal tests tailored to this specific data-generating process is a new methodological contribution.

proposals.title: LAD Estimation and Inference for Nonlinear Cointegrating Regressions with Locally Nonstationary Errors
proposals.motivation: Least Absolute Deviations (LAD) estimation is a robust alternative to least squares in cointegrating regressions, particularly in the presence of heavy-tailed errors. However, its established asymptotic theory is confined to linear conditional mean functions. In applied time-series econometrics, nonlinear cointegration relationships (e.g., smooth transition, polynomial) are increasingly relevant, but robust estimation theory for such models remains underdeveloped, especially when errors exhibit near-integrated or locally persistent behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: The LAD estimator for a class of nonlinear cointegrating functions, where the regressors are integrated processes, will converge at the standard n-rate and have a non-standard limiting distribution characterized by a function of Brownian motion and a local time process, even when the errors are driven by tempered or locally-to-unity processes.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of Phillips (1995) and subsequent LAD cointegration work. The approach involves: 1) Defining a general nonlinear conditional mean function m(I(1) regressor, θ) satisfying certain smoothness and identification conditions. 2) Employing a functional local limit theory to handle the nonlinearity, using the concept of local time of Brownian motion to characterize the asymptotic distribution. 3) Developing point optimal and Lagrange Multiplier tests for hypotheses on θ in this context, building on Phillips's recent work on optimal testing with local-to-unity roots. 4) Using simulation methods to approximate the non-standard limiting distributions for inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete asymptotic theory for robust LAD estimation of nonlinear cointegrating relationships, filling a significant gap between nonlinear cointegration and robust econometrics. It will deliver practical inference procedures (tests and confidence intervals) that are valid under broader error conditions (e.g., infinite variance, local nonstationarity) than standard nonlinear least squares.
proposals.novelty: The extension of LAD asymptotics to nonlinear cointegration is non-trivial, as the nonlinear transformation of an I(1) process necessitates a different probabilistic limit (involving local time) than the linear case. Combining this with a driving error process that is a tempered or locally stationary linear process, as in Phillips's recent work, creates a novel and technically challenging asymptotic problem.

proposals.title: Limit Theory for LAD Estimation with Nonstationary Regressors and Heavy-Tailed Errors
proposals.motivation: Least Absolute Deviations (LAD) estimation is a cornerstone of robust regression, offering resilience to heavy-tailed errors. However, its asymptotic theory is predominantly established for stationary data. In pre-2026 econometrics, the analysis of nonstationary processes (e.g., unit roots, near-integrated, cointegrated systems) is central, yet the behavior of LAD under such nonstationarity remains largely unexplored, creating a significant gap for robust time series modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LAD estimators for cointegrating relations or models with locally explosive regressors will converge at the super-consistent rate (n) but with a limiting distribution characterized by a functional of Brownian motion and the local time of the limit process, differing fundamentally from OLS limits due to the quantile criterion.
proposals.method: The approach builds on Phillips's recent work on near-integrated and functionally local-to-unity processes. We will derive the asymptotic distribution by first establishing a functional central limit theorem for the score process of the LAD objective function under general linear process errors. The key technical step involves using a uniform-in-parameter Bahadur representation and then applying continuous mapping to the resulting normalized objective function. The limit will be expressed using Brownian motion and local time, analyzed via simulation of the limiting distribution for critical values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive limit theory for LAD regression with nonstationary regressors, enabling valid inference in cointegrated systems with infinite variance errors. It will extend the toolbox of robust econometrics to nonstationary environments, directly complementing Phillips's research on optimal testing and estimation with persistent data.
proposals.novelty: While the limit theory for OLS with unit roots is classic, the analogous theory for LAD is non-trivial due to the non-differentiable objective function and leads to a distinct limiting distribution involving local time, a feature absent in OLS asymptotics. This merges the robust estimation and nonstationary time series literatures in a novel way.

proposals.title: Asymptotic Theory for High-Dimensional Covariance Matrix Testing Under Functional Local-to-Unity Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In modern econometrics and statistics, testing covariance matrices is fundamental for portfolio analysis, factor models, and signal detection. Pre-2026 literature on high-dimensional testing (e.g., John's test, Ledoit-Wolf corrections) typically assumes independent or stationary data, failing to account for the persistent, near-integrated time series prevalent in macroeconomic and financial datasets. This gap limits reliable inference in high-dimensional, non-stationary settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the framework of point optimal testing with functionally local-to-unity roots to a high-dimensional context where the matrix dimension p grows with sample size n, we can derive a non-degenerate asymptotic distribution for a class of covariance matrix test statistics, even under strong temporal dependence.
proposals.method: The approach builds on Phillips's recent work on point optimal tests and near-integrated processes. We will first embed the high-dimensional covariance matrix within a vector autoregressive framework with roots of the form ρ = 1 + c/n, where c is a localizing function. Using random matrix theory and high-dimensional probability limit theorems, we will analyze the joint limit as (n, p) → ∞ with p/n → γ ∈ (0, ∞). The core technical task is to establish the weak convergence of the sample covariance matrix's spectral statistics under this double asymptotic regime, adapting tools from sample covariance processes and functional limit theory.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first rigorous asymptotic theory for testing high-dimensional covariance matrices in the presence of near-unit root nonstationarity. It will yield practical, robust testing procedures for financial econometrics where dimensionality and persistence coexist. The results will bridge the fields of high-dimensional inference and nonstationary time series analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of Phillips's functional local-to-unity framework with high-dimensional double asymptotics, a combination not addressed in pre-2026 literature. Moving beyond i.i.d. or stationary assumptions to persistent, high-dimensional data presents significant and non-obvious theoretical challenges.

ideas.title: Joint Inference for Mean and Volatility Regimes in Univariate Threshold Autoregressive Models
ideas.description: This research develops a unified maximum likelihood framework for the specification and estimation of univariate self-exciting threshold autoregressive models that allow for concurrent shifts in both the conditional mean and conditional variance. The methodology provides formal statistical tests to distinguish between pure mean regimes, pure volatility regimes, and coupled mean-volatility regimes, overcoming the sequential testing problem. It introduces a novel model selection criterion that jointly determines the optimal number of lags and the existence/location of thresholds for both equations, ensuring parsimony. The approach delivers robust inference on regime-specific dynamics and volatility persistence, which is critical for accurate forecasting and risk assessment in financial and macroeconomic time series.

ideas.title: Empirical Detection of Structural Breaks in Cointegration Spaces for Macroeconomic Policy Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops and applies a unified empirical toolkit to detect structural breaks in cointegrating relationships within key macroeconomic datasets (e.g., money demand, fiscal sustainability, purchasing power parity). The core innovation is a methodology that iteratively applies robust rank selection to identify breakpoints, then uses the timing of these breaks to guide a narrative historical analysis. This synergy allows the statistical model to pinpoint potential regime shifts—such as changes in monetary policy anchors or fiscal rules—which are then investigated using historical policy records, institutional changes, and major economic events to establish plausible economic explanations, thereby bridging formal econometrics with applied macroeconomic history.

ideas.title: Joint Inference for Factor Rank and Volatility Regimes in High-Dimensional Time Series
ideas.description: This research develops a methodology for the simultaneous, joint estimation of the latent factor rank and multiple volatility break points in high-dimensional time series. Instead of a fragile two-step procedure, it employs a unified statistical model where a penalty term controls both the sparsity of factor loadings (for rank selection) and the number of structural breaks in the idiosyncratic variances. The framework provides a coherent mechanism for inference, yielding consistent estimators for both the number of factors and the break dates, along with valid measures of joint estimation uncertainty. This approach ensures robustness by directly modeling the feedback between determining the low-rank structure and identifying volatility regimes.

ideas.title: Derive limit distributions for other inconsistent criteria
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to characterize the asymptotic distributions of rank selection criteria that are known to be inconsistent (e.g., fail to select the true model with probability approaching 1) in the presence of time-varying variance in the data-generating process. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, specifically the analysis of neutral allele frequencies in populations with fluctuating size, we adapt the 'time-changed diffusion' solution. In that field, fluctuating population size is handled by applying a stochastic time change to the standard Wright-Fisher diffusion. Similarly, for inconsistent model selection criteria, the time-varying variance will be treated as a random process that warps the asymptotic time scale. The adapted research idea is to formally model the sequence of criterion values as a process whose martingale component undergoes a time change determined by the cumulative inverse variance. The solution will involve deriving the resulting limit distribution, which is expected to be non-Gaussian and described by a stochastic differential equation driven by this variance-induced time change, thereby providing a general characterization for a class of inconsistent criteria under heteroskedasticity.

ideas.title: Relax weak dependence to allow for stronger dependence
ideas.description: This research extends statistical and econometric theory by relaxing the common assumption of weak dependence in error processes to accommodate long memory or other forms of strong dependence. The analogy is drawn from geophysics, specifically seismology, where the Omori-Utsu law for aftershock sequences models events with inherent, long-range temporal dependence (a power-law decay) rather than assuming independence. Seismologists solved this by developing new probabilistic models (like epidemic-type aftershock sequences) that explicitly parameterize the decay and clustering strength. Adapted to this context, the solution involves developing a flexible modeling framework where the error process is specified with a parameter (e.g., a fractional integration parameter d or a power-law decay coefficient) that captures the spectrum from weak to strong dependence. Estimation and inference methods would then treat this dependence strength as a core model parameter to be estimated, allowing the theory and subsequent tests (like unit root or cointegration tests) to remain valid under a much broader class of data-generating processes, including long memory.

ideas.title: Robust Joint Selection and Inference Under Deterministic Model Uncertainty
ideas.description: This approach develops a semiparametric framework that explicitly accounts for uncertainty in the specification of deterministic components like trends and breaks. Instead of conditioning on a single deterministic model, the procedure conducts inference across a union of plausible deterministic specifications, using techniques like weighted averaging or intersection-union tests. This ensures the selection of the nonparametric or flexible component is robust to misspecification of the deterministic part. The result is a more reliable model selection and valid inference, even when the true form of the trend or break is unknown.

ideas.title: Dual: Spectral Gap Selection via Random Matrix Theory
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Statistical Physics and Random Matrix Theory to the semiparametric selection of the number of common stochastic trends. In complex systems, the effective dimensionality is determined by identifying a spectral gap in the eigenvalue distribution of a correlation matrix, separating signal from noise. We apply this analogically: from a multivariate time series, construct an appropriate covariance matrix and compute its eigenvalues. The number of eigenvalues that fall outside the distribution expected under a null hypothesis of no cointegration (e.g., by comparing to the Tracy-Widom distribution or a scaled Marchenko-Pastur law for time series) directly indicates the rank of the common trend space. This provides a dual, scaling-based selection method rooted in the spectral properties of the data's covariance structure.

ideas.title: Extend rank selection consistency to long memory errors via spectral domain adaptation
ideas.description: Generalize the consistency results for rank selection to models with long memory or persistent error components by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, consistent signal detection in the presence of long-memory seismic noise (modeled as fractional Brownian motion) is achieved by adapting model selection criteria to the frequency domain, explicitly accounting for the power-law spectral density. The adapted solution is to develop a frequency-domain information criterion (e.g., a modified BIC) for rank selection based on the Whittle likelihood approximation. This involves using a tapered periodogram of the residuals and incorporating a known or estimated spectral density function of the long-memory process into the likelihood, leading to a corrected penalty term that guarantees consistency of the rank estimator despite the strong temporal dependence in the errors.

ideas.title: Applying a Robust, Multi-Scale Validation Protocol from Ecology to Macroeconomic Cointegration
ideas.description: This expanded idea draws an analogy from Population Ecology, where Species Distribution Models (SDMs) are rigorously validated for practical utility. The analogous solution involves a multi-scale validation protocol, testing models across different spatial scales, with independent data, and using techniques like spatial block k-folding to ensure robustness and transferability. Adapted to macroeconomic cointegration research, this translates to designing a future work project that systematically demonstrates the method's practical convenience. The research would apply the cointegration method not to a single dataset, but across a battery of empirical tests: using macroeconomic data of varying temporal frequencies (e.g., quarterly vs. annual), partitioning data into distinct economic regimes (e.g., Great Moderation vs. post-Global Financial Crisis), and testing transferability between different economies or currency unions. This structured, ecology-inspired validation framework would provide a more comprehensive, convincing, and practically informative demonstration of the method's utility on real-world cointegration problems than a single case study.

ideas.title: Memory-Robust Rank Selection: Theory and Penalty Rescaling for Persistent Error Processes
ideas.description: This research provides a complete framework for consistent model order (rank) selection in the presence of errors exhibiting long-range dependence. It first derives the precise asymptotic bias introduced by persistent errors into standard information criteria, identifying where and why consistency fails. The core innovation is a robust selection procedure that explicitly estimates the error memory parameter and adapts the penalty term of the selection criterion accordingly. This results in a unified, theoretically grounded methodology that guarantees consistent rank estimation regardless of whether the underlying error process is short-memory, long-memory, or even anti-persistent.

ideas.title: Adapting Signal Decomposition for Semiparametric Model Selection
ideas.description: This idea proposes integrating deterministic trends or breaks into semiparametric model selection by drawing an analogy from signal processing. In that field, raw signals are decomposed into interpretable components like trend and noise before targeted analysis. The adapted solution involves a two-stage framework: first, identify and extract deterministic components (e.g., via parametric trend modeling or breakpoint detection). Second, apply the semiparametric selection procedure to the residual stochastic series, where standard smoothness or sparsity assumptions are more likely to hold. The final model explicitly combines the parametric deterministic component with the selected semiparametric model of the residuals, ensuring the selection mechanism is robust to the presence of strong non-stationary features.

ideas.title: A Diagnostic and Correction Framework for Rank Selection Under Model Misspecification
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework to diagnose, quantify, and correct the asymptotic failures of model rank selection criteria under common misspecifications like time-varying variance. The core theoretical advance is a sensitivity analysis that decomposes the limit distribution of any criterion into a stable component and a distortion term caused by the violation. This decomposition enables the construction of robust, corrected criteria and provides practitioners with diagnostic measures to assess the reliability of a chosen rank in finite samples. The output is both a theoretical characterization of failure and a practical toolkit for more reliable model selection in non-standard settings.

ideas.title: Adaptive Error Structure Selection for Robust Reduced-Rank Regression
ideas.description: This research develops a unified Reduced-Rank Regression (RRR) framework that adaptively selects the optimal error process specification from a set of candidate parametric and nonparametric models. The core innovation is an automated, criterion-based selection mechanism embedded within the estimation procedure, which penalizes model complexity to prevent overfitting. This approach ensures the chosen error structure is justified by the data rather than analyst discretion, significantly improving the robustness and reproducibility of inference. The method is validated through simulations and applied to datasets where misspecification of errors is a known concern.

ideas.title: Ensemble Volatility Filtering with Dynamic Model Selection for Robust Rank Estimation
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses parametric model fragility by employing an ensemble of competing volatility models, such as GARCH, EGARCH, stochastic volatility, and rolling window estimators. During the rank selection procedure, a model confidence set or adaptive weighting scheme dynamically selects or combines the most reliable volatility filter for the current data window. This approach ensures the rank selection is informed by a robust, data-adaptive measure of time-varying variance, significantly reducing bias from model misspecification. The result is a more reliable and generalizable methodology for rank determination in the presence of complex, evolving volatility dynamics.

ideas.title: Bootstrap Calibration of Rank Selection via Ecological Model Validation Analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from Ecology, specifically from species distribution model validation. In that field, where theoretical distributions for complex model selection are often unavailable, ecologists use resampling methods like spatial block bootstrap or cross-validation on observational data to generate empirical distributions of fit statistics, thereby assessing model robustness. Adapting this solution, we propose a paired bootstrap procedure for rank selection in cointegrated systems or factor models. For each bootstrap sample drawn from the original data (with care to preserve serial and cross-sectional dependencies), the vector error correction model (or factor model) is estimated for a range of possible ranks, and a selection criterion (e.g., the trace statistic, IC(p)) is computed. The collection of these bootstrapped criterion values across many replications forms an empirical finite-sample distribution. This distribution can be used to set calibrated critical values or to compute selection probabilities for each rank, providing a more reliable finite-sample inference tool than asymptotic approximations alone.

ideas.title: Double Bootstrap Calibration for Robust Finite-Sample Rank Inference Under Heteroskedasticity
ideas.description: This approach introduces a double (bootstrapping the bootstrap) calibration framework to overcome the finite-sample sensitivity of wild bootstrap for rank tests. The inner loop performs a standard wild bootstrap to approximate the test statistic's distribution, while the outer loop evaluates and corrects the error in this approximation by resampling from the observed data. This calibration step yields second-order accurate p-values and confidence intervals that are robust to the choice of wild bootstrap weights and complex heteroskedasticity. The method provides theoretically justified, simulation-validated inference for rank-based procedures in small to moderate samples where standard wild bootstrap may fail.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Resampling for Heteroskedastic Rank Tests
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from ecology, where null model resampling tests are used to infer significant patterns in species distributions while preserving key data constraints. The analogous solution involves randomizing data under a specific null hypothesis but keeping certain marginal totals or structures fixed to reflect real-world conditions. Adapted to the original econometric/statistical problem, this translates to developing a wild bootstrap technique for finite-sample inference on rank test statistics under heteroskedasticity. The adapted method would explicitly treat the observed heteroskedastic pattern as a fixed constraint (similar to fixed row/column sums in an ecological matrix). During the bootstrap data generation process, this heteroskedastic structure is preserved, and wild bootstrap multipliers are applied to simulate the sampling distribution of the rank statistic under the null hypothesis. This approach aims to provide more accurate p-values and confidence intervals in finite samples where traditional asymptotic approximations may fail due to heterogeneous variances.

ideas.title: A Diagnostic and Tiered Inference Framework for Strongly Dependent Processes
ideas.description: This research develops a principled, two-stage framework for regression with potentially strongly dependent errors. The first stage introduces a rigorous diagnostic procedure, based on local Whittle estimation or wavelet techniques, to classify the nature and strength of the dependence present in the model residuals. The second stage employs a tiered library of inference methods, each with proven theoretical properties (e.g., rate-optimality, validity) for a specific class of dependence identified by the diagnostic. The outcome is a robust, automated system that moves beyond assumption relaxation to provide guaranteed, context-appropriate inference under a wide spectrum of dependence.

ideas.title: Extend limit theory for semiparametric RRR using tensor decomposition methods from chemometrics
ideas.description: This research generalizes the limit theory for semiparametric reduced rank regression to models with multiple lags by drawing an analogy from chemometrics. In chemometrics, the analysis of multi-way data arrays (e.g., excitation-emission fluorescence matrices over time) with multiple dimensions is addressed using tensor decomposition methods like parallel factor analysis (PARAFAC) and Tucker decompositions. These methods extend low-rank matrix factorization to higher-order tensors, providing identifiable models and established convergence properties. We adapt this solution by treating the multiple lag RRR problem as a third-order tensor (observations × predictors × lags) and imposing a reduced rank structure on the coefficient array via a PARAFAC or Tucker model. The adapted research idea involves developing a limit theory for the resulting semiparametric estimator, ensuring identifiability conditions derived from the tensor decomposition literature, and establishing the asymptotic distribution of the parameter estimates in this extended framework.

ideas.title: Asymptotic Distribution of Cross-Validated Penalty Sequences for Inconsistent Model Selection
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the asymptotic properties of the penalty coefficient C_n when it is selected via a data-driven cross-validation procedure, rather than being a fixed sequence. By characterizing the limit distribution of the cross-validated C_n under conditions where the target criterion (e.g., BIC, Hannan-Quinn) is inconsistent for model selection, it reveals how the randomness from resampling propagates to the final selected model. This provides a unified theoretical framework to assess and compare the finite-sample variability and asymptotic reliability of adaptive penalty selection methods, going beyond the pointwise analysis of fixed penalty sequences.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Aftershock Models for Explosive Volatility Regimes
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of mean-reverting or stationary volatility by proposing a model where volatility itself can exhibit unit root or explosive behavior through self-exciting mechanisms. Drawing an analogy from seismology, specifically the solution to modeling nonstationary sequences of earthquakes via the Epidemic-Type Aftershock Sequence (ETAS) model, we adapt its self-exciting point process framework. The adapted research idea is to model financial volatility as a branching process where each volatility shock increases the conditional intensity of future shocks, creating cascading feedback. This provides a formal structure to capture explosive volatility regimes where large market moves beget further instability, moving beyond traditional autoregressive volatility models to ones where the variance process can have a unit root or explosive root driven by endogenous excitation.

ideas.title: Regulated Explosive Volatility Models with Stochastic Bounds
ideas.description: This approach models volatility dynamics that can enter explosive or strongly nonstationary regimes, mimicking market crises, but crucially couples this with a stochastic bounding process to prevent unconditional divergence. The system uses a two-component structure: an inner process that can exhibit locally explosive behavior and an outer regulatory process (e.g., a mean-reverting volatility-of-volatility process) that dampens or contains the inner process before forecasts become infinite. This allows for superior in-sample fit during turbulent periods while generating well-behaved, finite multi-step ahead forecasts for risk management. Estimation and inference are performed using Bayesian methods with priors that enforce the bounding mechanism, ensuring model identifiability and stability.

ideas.title: Cross-Validated Rank Selection for High-Dimensional, Low-Sample-Size Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified methodology for rank selection in very high-dimensional systems (p >> n) by integrating it with a cross-validation procedure for penalty parameter selection. The core innovation is a computationally efficient algorithm that uses data-driven cross-validation to simultaneously tune the penalty coefficient C_n and identify the optimal model rank in finite samples. This addresses the critical limitation of traditional rank selection methods, which rely on asymptotic criteria that break down when p >> n, by providing a practical, sample-adaptive estimation strategy. The resulting framework enables reliable low-rank structure discovery in complex, data-scarce environments like genomics and financial modeling.

ideas.title: Adapting Distributed Computing Checkpointing for Large Panel Cointegration
ideas.description: The core challenge is extending semiparametric methods to large-N, large-T panel cointegration models, where computational complexity and memory requirements become prohibitive. Drawing an analogy from High-Performance Computing, specifically the 'checkpoint-restart' mechanism used in distributed systems for large-scale simulations, we adapt this solution. The analogous solution involves periodically saving computational state to enable fault tolerance and manageable partitioning of massive problems. The adapted research idea is to develop a checkpoint-restart framework for the iterative estimation algorithms used in semiparametric panel cointegration. This framework would allow the estimation process for massive datasets to be broken into sequential or distributed chunks, with periodic saving of sufficient statistics, kernel weights, or gradient states. This enables scalable computation by overcoming single-machine memory limits, facilitates the use of distributed computing clusters, and provides fault tolerance for long-running estimations, making the analysis of very large panel datasets computationally feasible.

ideas.title: Phase Transitions in Rank Selection Under Non-Vanishing Regularization Asymptotics
ideas.description: This research systematically characterizes the behavior of rank-selection mechanisms when the penalty coefficient's growth rate violates the classical condition C_n/n → 0, a regime typically avoided in theoretical analysis. We establish a rigorous high-dimensional asymptotic framework where n, p, and C_n grow together, focusing on convex penalization methods like the nuclear norm. The core deliverable is a precise map of phase transitions: identifying critical thresholds for lim(C_n/n) where the estimator's rank transitions from being consistent to biased/undershrunken, and finally to zero. This provides a complete theoretical understanding of regularization outside the standard regime and offers practical guidance for tuning when strong shrinkage is intentionally desired.

ideas.title: A Decision-Theoretic Framework for Model Selection Using the Joint Asymptotics of Information Criteria Under Misspecification
ideas.description: This research develops a prescriptive framework for model selection by deriving the joint asymptotic distribution of a suite of information criteria (AIC, BIC, HQIC, etc.) under conditions of potential model misspecification. Using this joint distribution, the work constructs formal procedures to: (1) test for significant differences between models selected by different criteria, (2) compute the finite-sample risk and selection error probabilities associated with each criterion, and (3) generate a combined model weight or selection recommendation that robustly incorporates the evidence from all criteria. The output is a practical toolkit that moves beyond theoretical characterization to provide applied researchers with statistically grounded methods for resolving conflicts between selection criteria.

ideas.title: Structured Low-Rank Inference for High-Dimensional, Small-Sample Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a principled framework for low-rank inference when the number of variables vastly exceeds the sample size. It moves beyond generic rank selection by formally embedding the problem within a specified statistical model that includes structural assumptions on both the signal and noise. The methodology jointly estimates the model parameters and the effective rank using regularization techniques that ensure stability and identifiability. The outcome is a robust procedure with provable guarantees for specific tasks like subspace estimation or prediction, making it applicable to fields like genomics and neuroimaging.

ideas.title: Derive limit distributions for inconsistent criteria via universality classes
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution framework from statistical physics, where diverse systems are grouped into universality classes based on their asymptotic scaling behavior near a critical point. The analogous problem is characterizing the limit distributions of various inconsistent information criteria (beyond AIC) under model misspecification. Instead of deriving distributions criterion-by-criterion, we propose to group criteria into universality classes defined by how their penalty term scales with sample size (e.g., log(n), n^α, etc.). For each class, we identify key scaling variables and derive the asymptotic distribution for a canonical representative. This approach provides a powerful, unified characterization: knowing the class of a criterion immediately informs its limit behavior, significantly generalizing results from individual derivations like those for AIC.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Model Selection to Univariate Threshold Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical model selection framework from computational phylogenetics to the problem of rank selection in univariate threshold models. In phylogenetics, analysts select among a nested hierarchy of nucleotide substitution models (e.g., from simple JC69 to complex GTR+Γ+I) using information criteria or sequential likelihood ratio tests to find the model that best explains the data without over-parameterization. Analogously, for a univariate TAR or STAR process with potential volatility breaks, we can define a nested model hierarchy based on the number of mean regimes (thresholds) and variance states (volatility breaks). The adapted solution involves developing a sequential testing procedure—akin to phylogenetic model selection—that uses a modified information criterion or a suite of tests to jointly and consistently select the optimal number of thresholds and volatility breaks. This provides a rigorous, automated framework for 'scaling down' complex multivariate rank selection logic to the univariate case, determining the correct model dimension for both the conditional mean and variance.

ideas.title: Incorporate Adaptive Filtering from Signal Processing into GARCH-based Rank Selection
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from signal processing. The analogous problem is estimating a time-varying system (like a communication channel) in a non-stationary noise environment, solved using adaptive filtering algorithms that recursively update parameters and their uncertainties. Adapted to the research context, we propose a method where rank selection is not a one-time procedure but a continuous, adaptive process. A joint model integrates a low-rank matrix factorization with a GARCH process on the residuals. The algorithm recursively updates both the factor matrices and the GARCH parameters. The time-varying variance estimate from the GARCH component then directly informs the certainty of the current rank estimate, enabling dynamic rank selection that adapts to periods of high or low volatility in the underlying data-generating process.

ideas.title: Joint Bayesian Inference of Change-Points and Segment-Specific Latent Rank
ideas.description: This method proposes a unified Bayesian model that jointly infers temporal change-points and the intrinsic low-rank dimension of data within each resulting segment. Instead of a sequential pipeline, it defines a generative process where data in each hypothesized segment is drawn from a factor model with a segment-specific rank, and change-points are governed by a prior like a Markov switching process. A structured variational inference algorithm is developed to simultaneously approximate the posterior over change-points and the latent factors, coupling the uncertainty in segmentation with the uncertainty in rank. This integrated approach prevents error propagation, provides uncertainty quantification for both structural breaks and rank, and yields more robust adaptive dimensionality reduction for non-stationary matrix sequences.

ideas.title: Adaptive Error Process Specification via Model Selection and Ensemble Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from climate modeling, where ensembles of models with different physical parameterizations are used to manage structural uncertainty. The analogous solution involves running multiple models and using selection or averaging techniques like Bayesian Model Averaging. Adapted to the RRR context, this means relaxing the rigid semiparametric error assumption by proposing a methodological framework that treats the error process specification as a model choice problem. The framework would allow for a fully parametric, alternative nonparametric, or a mix of error specifications. For a given dataset, statistical criteria (e.g., AIC, BIC) or likelihood-based weighting schemes would be employed to automatically select the most appropriate error model or to create a combined, robust estimator from an ensemble, thereby improving inference and prediction by directly addressing and incorporating uncertainty about the error structure.

ideas.description: This research fuses bootstrap methods with machine learning regularization to develop a novel inference procedure for rank selection. It involves applying a nuclear norm or LASSO penalty to induce low-rank structure, then using a tailored bootstrap (e.g., residual or paired bootstrap) to approximate the sampling distribution of the selected rank across the regularization path. This allows for quantifying the uncertainty of the selected rank in finite samples, such as computing the probability that the estimated rank equals the true rank. The synergy provides a statistically rigorous, data-driven tool for model selection confidence in low-rank matrix estimation problems.

It jointly optimizes over both the locations of rank-change points and the low-rank matrices within each segment, using a regularization path that is adaptive to the inferred temporal structure. This allows for automatic segmentation of data streams or time-series matrices into regimes of constant rank, with sparsity-inducing penalties ensuring parsimonious rank selection within each regime. The result is a unified framework for analyzing high-dimensional, time-varying data where the complexity of the underlying model can change abruptly.
