ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where change-point detection is used to segment seismic signals into distinct geological layers before estimating layer properties. The analogous problem in data analysis is selecting a single model rank for non-stationary data streams. The adapted solution is to first use change-point detection algorithms (like PELT or CUSUM) on a streaming measure related to data dimensionality (e.g., sequential singular values) to partition the data into temporally homogeneous segments. Within each statistically stationary segment, a conventional rank selection criterion is then applied independently, allowing for adaptive, data-driven rank selection that evolves with the underlying structure of the data, improving the accuracy of subsequent matrix decompositions or model fits.

Multiple random splits of the data are generated, and a penalty parameter is selected via cross-validation on each split. The distribution of these selected penalties is analyzed to identify a stable, high-probability region or to compute a robust aggregate (e.g., a trimmed mean). The final penalty is chosen from this robust consensus, ensuring the selection is less dependent on any single arbitrary data partition and significantly improving reproducibility and estimation stability.

In ecology, criteria like AICc are used to select the optimal SAR model complexity by penalizing the likelihood based on the number of parameters, preventing overfitting. We adapt this by framing rank selection as a penalized estimation problem where the penalty term is explicitly designed to mimic the small-sample-size correction and parameter-count penalty of ecological model selection. The solution applies a structured regularization penalty directly to the singular values during the optimization of a matrix/tensor decomposition, automatically shrinking unnecessary rank components in a data-adaptive way that accounts for model complexity and sample size, analogous to how ecologists balance fit and complexity when modeling biodiversity.

ideas.title: Adapting Compressed Sensing for Rank Selection in High-Dimensional, Low-Sample Systems
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from signal processing. The analogous problem in compressed sensing involves recovering a high-dimensional sparse signal from very few measurements, solved via L1-norm minimization. We adapt this solution to the context of rank selection for systems where the number of variables (p) exceeds the sample size (n). The core adaptation is to treat the desired low-rank matrix (e.g., a covariance or coefficient matrix) as the 'signal' and the limited sample data as the 'measurements'. By formulating rank selection as a nuclear norm minimization problem—promoting low-rank solutions similarly to how L1-norm promotes sparsity—we aim to develop a method that can reliably identify the effective rank in these high-dimensional, low-sample-size regimes, overcoming the limitations of traditional criteria that fail when p >> n.

ideas.title: Apply K-fold cross-validation for penalty selection, inspired by model tuning in machine learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established K-fold cross-validation procedure from machine learning for hyperparameter tuning to the statistical problem of selecting a penalty coefficient C_n in finite samples. In the analogous field, this method robustly prevents overfitting by repeatedly evaluating a model's performance on different held-out data subsets. The adapted solution involves defining a grid of candidate C_n values, iteratively fitting the penalized model on K-1 folds of the data, evaluating its performance on the remaining validation fold using a pre-specified loss function, and averaging the results to choose the C_n that generalizes best. This provides a fully data-driven, computationally intensive but practical method for penalty selection that leverages internal validation to approximate out-of-sample performance.
